source.title: token-econ-regional-sensitivity-lab
source.body:
Objective
---
Quick experimental workbook to translate regional GPU-hour prices and utilization profiles into $/1M-tokens estimates (hosted, pay-as-you-go baseline). This is a lightweight scenario lab (not the final canonical model) intended for infra/product tradeoff conversations and to seed a canonical spreadsheet.

High-level approach (formula)
---
1) baseline_tokens_per_gpu_hour = throughput_tokens_per_sec * 3600
2) gpu_hours_per_1M_tokens = 1_000_000 / baseline_tokens_per_gpu_hour
3) base_cost_per_1M = gpu_hours_per_1M_tokens * list_price_per_gpu_hour_by_region
4) final_cost_per_1M = base_cost_per_1M * (1 / effective_utilization) * infra_overhead_multiplier * (1 - cache_hit_rate) * reserved_discount

Assumptions used in this lab (conservative midpoints)
---
- Representative GPUs and steady-state throughput (tokens/sec):
  - H100: 12,000 tps
  - A100: 6,000 tps
  - L4: 2,500 tps
- Implied tokens/hour: H100=43.2M, A100=21.6M, L4=9.0M
- infra_overhead_multiplier = 1.08 (8% overhead: network, multi-tenancy ineff, orchestration buffers)
- cache_hit_rate = 20% (effective token reduction from prefix/KV caching)
- reserved_discount = 1.00 (hosted pay-as-you-go baseline). We include a note on reserved pricing later.
- utilization scenarios considered: low=40% (lots of headroom/queue gaps), mid=70% (typical after batching improvements), high=95% (very tight, high efficiency)

Core derived numbers (gpu_hours_per_1M_tokens and base $/1M)
---
| GPU  | tokens/hr | gpu_hours_per_1M | base $/1M (us-west1 price) | notes |
|------|-----------:|------------------:|--------------------------:|-------|
| H100 | 43,200,000 | 0.023148 hrs      | $0.1667 (price $7.20/hr)  | highest throughput, best base $/1M |
| A100 | 21,600,000 | 0.046296 hrs      | $0.1760 (price $3.80/hr)  | mid throughput |
| L4   | 9,000,000  | 0.111111 hrs      | $0.2000 (price $1.80/hr)  | cheaper per-hour but lower throughput |

Regional list-price per GPU-hour used in this lab (examples)
---
| region       | H100 ($/hr) | A100 ($/hr) | L4 ($/hr) |
|--------------|------------:|------------:|----------:|
| us-west1     | 7.20        | 3.80        | 1.80      |
| us-central1  | 6.40        | 3.40        | 1.60      |
| europe-west1 | 8.10        | 4.20        | 2.10      |
| asia-east1   | 9.50        | 4.90        | 2.40      |

Sensitivity table: $/1M tokens by GPU, region, utilization (cache_hit_rate=20%, infra_overhead=1.08)
---
Combined multipliers for convenience: low(40%) => 2.16x, mid(70%) => 1.2343x, high(95%) => 0.908x (these include 1/utilization * infra_overhead * (1-cache_rate)).

H100 ($/1M)
| region       | base | low (40%) | mid (70%) | high (95%) |
|--------------|------:|----------:|----------:|-----------:|
| us-west1     | 0.1667| 0.360    | 0.206    | 0.151     |
| us-central1  | 0.1482| 0.320    | 0.183    | 0.135     |
| europe-west1 | 0.1878| 0.406    | 0.232    | 0.171     |
| asia-east1   | 0.2209| 0.477    | 0.273    | 0.201     |

A100 ($/1M)
| region       | base | low (40%) | mid (70%) | high (95%) |
|--------------|------:|----------:|----------:|-----------:|
| us-west1     | 0.1760| 0.380    | 0.217    | 0.160     |
| us-central1  | 0.1576| 0.340    | 0.195    | 0.143     |
| europe-west1 | 0.1944| 0.420    | 0.240    | 0.177     |
| asia-east1   | 0.2268| 0.491    | 0.280    | 0.206     |

L4 ($/1M)
| region       | base | low (40%) | mid (70%) | high (95%) |
|--------------|------:|----------:|----------:|-----------:|
| us-west1     | 0.2000| 0.432    | 0.247    | 0.182     |
| us-central1  | 0.1778| 0.384    | 0.220    | 0.162     |
| europe-west1 | 0.2333| 0.504    | 0.289    | 0.213     |
| asia-east1   | 0.2667| 0.576    | 0.331    | 0.242     |

Interpretation / quick notes
---
- Across these baseline assumptions, per-1M-token costs (hosted, after a modest 20% cache hit) fall in roughly $0.15–$0.58 depending on GPU type, region, and utilization profile.
- Utilization is the single biggest lever. Moving from 40% -> 70% utilization drops $/1M by ~40–45%. Investing in batching and request consolidation often yields faster wins than chasing marginal GPU-hour price reductions.
- Regional price differences matter at scale. Asia regions show ~20–30% higher $/1M in this sample, primarily driven by list-price differences.
- For small models / low-latency use-cases where you can't heavily batch, cheaper-per-hour GPUs (L4) can still be more expensive per token. L4 only becomes attractive when sustained cache hits or model quantization significantly raises tokens/hr.

Reserve/commit effects and runbook implications
---
- Reserved/dedicated capacity: assume committed discount ~15% (reserved_discount ~ 0.85). That discount compounds: e.g., H100 us-west1 mid-util $/1M moves from $0.206 -> $0.175 after commit.
- Overcommit and fractional tenancy: if we can safely overcommit (multi-tenant packing) without tail-latency regressions, effective utilization improves which reduces $/1M more than a small list-price discount.

Cache sensitivity mini-table (H100 us-west1, mid-util 70%)
| cache_hit_rate | final $/1M |
|----------------:|-----------:|
| 0%             | 0.257      | (no caching)
| 20%            | 0.206      | (baseline in this doc)
| 40%            | 0.154      | (aggressive caching)

Measurement plan (short)
---
1) Benchmark tokens/sec per GPU for the top 6 candidate models with our serving runtime (run head-to-head on H100 and A100 with real prompts). Add a telemetry tag in production to capture observed tokens/sec and per-request sequence-length distribution. (Owner: eng-serving-runtime; JIRA: INFRA-TP-62)
2) Instrument live utilization by queue-delay and idle-gap metrics (owner: SRE). We want 30/60/90 day histograms of utilization by pool and region. (JIRA: SRE-Util-17)
3) Pilot: enable prefix cache for a high-traffic hosted API route (expected 20–40% hit). Measure delta in GPU-hours and p50/p95 latency. (Owner: product-hosted-api + eng-infrastructure)
4) Run reserve vs payg experiment for a single EU-heavy customer: 6-month bench with 15% commit. Capture $/1M and SLA impacts.

Actionable next steps (this doc is a lab, not final)
---
- Convert this scenario lab into the canonical capacity workbook (sheet) with live-linked telemetry.
- Prioritize telemetry items so utilization and tokens/sec are first-class metrics in the console dashboards.
- Run a small A/B on batching improvements (increase batch windows for background routes) to quantify utilization gains and $/1M impact.

Open questions / caveats
---
- Throughput numbers above are conservative estimates and model-variant dependent (quantized vs fp16, KV cache effects). Replace with runtime bench results.
- This sheet assumes per-hour list price maps cleanly to customer cost; additional infra-level amortization (control plane, storage, egress) should be added for product-level chargebacks.
- Tail-latency constraints may force lower utilization in some routes — those routes should be modeled separately.

Notes on provenance
---
Created as a quicklab by Ethan Park (eng-infrastructure) with input from Marlana Silva and Noah Kim. For the canonical version, see CONFLUENCE-Infra/TokenEcon-Lab and linked GitHub PR for the final sheet template.

source.title: Replica skew and adaptive interconnect scheduling
source.content:
Summary:
This notes doc captures experiments and hypotheses around replica-level load skew interacting with the interconnect (NVLink/NVSwitch/PCIe) and NCCL collective choices. We observed recurring tail-latency and throughput loss when one or two replicas in a replica-group become heavier due to request imbalance or KV-cache replay spikes. Aim is to record tests, quick reproductions, and candidate mitigations for scheduling and runtime heuristics.

Background / motivation:
- Redwood serving runtime relies on a mix of tensor-parallel (TP) and pipeline-parallel (PP) shards across multiple GPUs. For multi-replica deployments we expect identical workload per replica, but in production skew happens (prefix-cache replays, cache-miss storms, user-bursting).
- When one replica is heavier the interconnect and collective algorithms expose imbalance: NCCL allreduce/allgather waits, increased host staging, and NVSwitch congestion cascades to other replicas causing p99 spikes.
- We need pragmatic mitigations (scheduling, backpressure, NCCL tuning) that are safe to deploy for hosted and dedicated offerings.

Testbed details:
- HW: 8x A100-80GB per host, NVSwitch full-mesh, Mellanox HDR IB between hosts. Also tested on 4x A100 on a DGX-like host and a 4x-GPU PCIe-only rack for comparison.
- SW: CUDA 12.1, NCCL 2.18, kernel 6.2, runtime-native serving harness (local harness branch: GH-PR-3210).
- Model: 7B TPx2 + PPx4 (logical split used for most experiments), seq len 1024 for synthetic workload, and a set of 100 customer prompts with varied prefix reuse for production-like runs.
- Metrics collected: per-GPU compute util, NCCL bandwidth per link (via nccl-tests and nvlinkperf), host-to-device transfer rates, per-request p50/p95/p99 latency, throughput (tokens/s), and queue depths at runtime.

Experiment 1: single-replica overload injection (intra-host)
- Procedure: fire steady stream of requests to 3 replicas, inject additional 2x traffic to replica-2 for 60s. Repeat across replicas.
- Observations:
  - Replica-2 shows expected CPU/GPU load increase. Shortly after, NCCL allreduce windows show 15–25% higher latency for the group (not just overloaded replica).
  - NVSwitch telemetry indicates increased packet queues on links adjacent to replica-2; other GPUs report occasional retransmits.
  - p99 across group increased from 42ms baseline to 110–160ms spikes during overload. p50 unaffected.
- Quick hypothesis: collective progress depends on slowest participant; congestion on links near the heavy replica delays the entire collective. Host staging (PCIe) also increased because host had to service more ephemeral KV-cache misses.

Experiment 2: selective NCCL algorithm forcing
- Procedure: set NCCL_ALGO=Ring vs NCCL_ALGO=Tree and measure. Also tested NCCL_MIN_NRINGS tweak and NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME pinning.
- Observations:
  - Ring performed slightly better for balanced loads but worsened tail under skew (p99 ~140ms). Tree had better tail behavior (p99 ~95ms) in several runs but sometimes reduced throughput by 8–12% for balanced runs.
  - Pinning RDMA/NIC interfaces reduced cross-host variability but didn't fix intrahost NVSwitch queueing.
- Interpretation: no single static NCCL algorithm is best. Adaptive selection by the runtime based on observed link utilization might help.

Experiment 3: micro-batching and chunk sizing sweep
- Procedure: probe chunk sizes for weight sharding and tensor chunking (4MB -> 256KB). Observe NCCL op sizes and frequency.
- Observations:
  - Smaller chunk sizes increase NCCL op count but reduce single-op completion time; this attenuated tail in some skewed cases by allowing faster stragglers to catch up incrementally.
  - Too-small chunks increase CPU overhead and host staging bursts; identified sweet spot around 512KB–1MB for our model/config.

Experiment 4: asynchronous-replica-progressive-commit (proof-of-concept)
- Idea: let replicas proceed a bounded number of micro-steps ahead and use an opportunistic small cross-replica reconciliation window to reduce head-of-line blocking.
- Procedure: implemented a simple POC where if a replica is delayed by >X ms, other replicas continue for up to N micro-steps and attach a catch-up token for later merging. This was done only for non-critical allreduce ops (KV-cache replay metadata) in POC harness.
- Observations:
  - Reduced p99 by ~20–35% in tests with mild skew. For severe skew this introduced correctness complexity and occasional stalling during the reconciliation phase.
  - Good candidate for KV-cache and prefix-only operations where perfect sync is not required. Not safe for weight-sync reduction ops.

Production incident reproduction attempt (case INF-221):
- Incident: p99 tail-latency bursts for a hosted model after a customer launched a campaign. Hypothesized KV-cache replay storm to a small subset of replicas.
- Reproduction: replayed campaign trace; reproduced p99 spikes matching production telemetry. The replica skew aligned with a frequent prefix that hit local cache eviction pattern.
- Mitigation applied in-prod: rate-limited incoming requests per replica (soft throttle) + forced NCCL_TREE for group while we rotated kernels. Immediately reduced p99 by ~60%.

Key takeaways / heuristics:
- Replica skew amplifies interconnect-sensitive failure modes: NVSwitch link saturation, PCIe host-staging spikes, and NCCL collective stalls.
- Static NCCL algorithm choice is a poor long-term strategy; runtime should select algorithm based on (a) measured per-link queuing, (b) op size distribution, and (c) cross-replica variance.
- Chunk sizing for TP shards has a measurable effect on tail: use adaptive chunking that increases chunk count when link latency variability is high, but cap to avoid CPU/host pressure.
- Opportunistic asynchronous micro-progress helps for non-critical sync burdens (cache, metadata) but must be gated and auditable.

Short list of candidate runtime changes (prioritized):
1) telemetry-driven NCCL policy: probe link utilization and pick algorithm per-alloc group. Low-risk: start with canary per-cluster; metric: p99 reduction without throughput regression.
2) replica-aware request steering: simple first step => soft per-replica concurrency caps + global load shed for replicas crossing threshold. Medium risk operationally but easy rollback.
3) adaptive chunker: adjust TP chunk size at shard start based on recent link latency variance. Low-medium risk.
4) micro-async for cache ops: feature flag gated, only for KV/cache metadata. High risk for correctness; short-term POC only.
5) NVSwitch-aware placement: shift hot replicas to top-of-rack placements with fewer hop distances when possible. High operational complexity.

Quick reproducible commands / harness notes:
- run nccl-tests to get baseline per-link bandwidth: ./build/all_reduce_perf -b 8 -e 512M -f 2 -g 8
- nvlink perf telemetry: nvlinkperf --device=0 --duration=60
- capturing NCCL traces: export NCCL_DEBUG=INFO; run harness; collect logs under /var/log/serve-profiling/<run-id>
- to force algorithm: export NCCL_ALGO=Tree; export NCCL_MIN_NRINGS=1
- harness branch: GH-PR-3210 has the stress injectors and extra metrics endpoints (prom compatible).

Open questions and follow-ups:
- How to compute safe bounds for micro-async without risking model divergence? Need a small formal spec and unit tests against model outputs.
- Can we instrument NCCL or driver to get per-op queue depth for NVSwitch links? Current telemetry is coarse. Follow up with infra team (ticket JIRA-INF-842).
- Evaluate interplay with quantized kernels where compute time is shorter — does that hide or amplify interconnect imbalance?
- Explore per-replica token bucket rate-limiting in the gateway to avoid producer storms.

Next steps (owners + timeline):
- (Ethan) Implement telemetry-driven NCCL selector POC, due 2025-02-07.
- (Maya) Build per-replica soft concurrency limiter in the gateway, due 2025-01-31.
- (Liam) Add micro-chunk auto-tuner testbed and run sweep across model sizes, due 2025-02-14.
- (Zhang) Work with infra on finer-grained NVSwitch telemetry, escalate JIRA-INF-842, due 2025-02-21.

Notes to self / rough ideas:
- Consider exposing an LED/metric in Console called "replica-skew-index" (0..1) for operations to quickly see skew across replica groups.
- For Dedicated customers consider an optional "balanced-arena" placement policy (replica placement affinity) to avoid hot-spotting across NVSwitch boundaries.

Appendix: quick log excerpt (anonymized)
- 2024-11-08T15:32:01Z replica-2: kv-replay=214rps host-staging=1.8GB/s nvlink-qlen=12 allreduce-ms=32
- 2024-11-08T15:32:04Z replica-1: kv-replay=12rps host-staging=0.2GB/s nvlink-qlen=2 allreduce-ms=28
- 2024-11-08T15:32:20Z NCCL: op(allreduce) size=4MB duration=13ms participants=8 algo=ring
- 2024-11-08T15:32:21Z NCCL: op(allreduce) size=4MB duration=47ms participants=8 algo=ring (retransmit suspected)

References / links:
- GH-PR-3210: POC harness with injection and metrics endpoints
- JIRA-INF-842: NVSwitch telemetry request
- confluence://doc/serving-runtime/tp-pp-rfcs : collection of prior TP/PP notes (see section "collectives-behavior")

title: Replica skew and adaptive interconnect scheduling
preview:
Summary:
This notes doc captures experiments and hypotheses around replica-level load skew interacting with the interconnect (NVLink/NVSwitch/PCIe) and NCCL collective choices. We observed recurring tail-latency and throughput loss when one or two replicas in a replica-group become heavier due to request imbalance or KV-cache replay spikes. Aim is to record tests, quick reproductions, and candidate mitigations for scheduling and runtime heuristics.

Background / motivation:
- Redwood serving runtime relies on a mix of tensor-parallel (TP) and pipeline-parallel (PP) shards across multiple GPUs. For multi-replica deployments we expect identical workload per replica, but in production skew happens (prefix-cache replays, cache-miss storms, user-bursting).
- When one replica is heavier the interconnect and collective algorithms expose imbalance: NCCL allreduce/allgather waits, increased host staging, and NVSwitch congestion cascades to other replicas causing p99 spikes.
- We need pragmatic mitigations (scheduling, backpressure, NCCL tuning) that are safe to deploy for hosted and dedicated offerings.

Testbed details:
- HW: 8x A100-80GB per host, NVSwitch full-mesh, Mellanox HDR IB between hosts. Also tested on 4x A100 on a DGX-like host and a 4x-GPU PCIe-only rack for comparison.
- SW: CUDA 12.1, NCCL 2.18, kernel 6.2, runtime-native serving harness (local harness branch: GH-PR-3210).
- Model: 7B TPx2 + PPx4 (logical split used for most experiments), seq len 1024 for synthetic workload, and a set of 100 customer prompts with varied prefix reuse for production-like runs.
- Metrics collected: per-GPU compute util, NCCL bandwidth per link (via nccl-tests and nvlinkperf), host-to-device transfer rates, per-request p50/p95/p99 latency, throughput (tokens/s), and queue depths at runtime.

Experiment 1: single-replica overload injection (intra-host)
- Procedure: fire steady stream of requests to 3 replicas, inject additional 2x traffic to replica-2 for 60s. Repeat across replicas.
- Observations:
  - Replica-2 shows expected CPU/GPU load increase. Shortly after, NCCL allreduce windows show 15–25% higher latency for the group (not just overloaded replica).
  - NVSwitch telemetry indicates increased packet queues on links adjacent to replica-2; other GPUs report occasional retransmits.
  - p99 across group increased from 42ms baseline to 110–160ms spikes during overload. p50 unaffected.
- Quick hypothesis: collective progress depends on slowest participant; congestion on links near the heavy replica delays the entire collective. Host staging (PCIe) also increased because host had to service more ephemeral KV-cache misses.

Experiment 2: selective NCCL algorithm forcing
- Procedure: set NCCL_ALGO=Ring vs NCCL_ALGO=Tree and measure. Also tested NCCL_MIN_NRINGS tweak and NCCL_SOCKET_IFNAME pinning.
- Observations:
  - Ring performed slightly better for balanced loads but worsened tail under skew (p99 ~140ms). Tree had better tail behavior (p99 ~95ms) in several runs but sometimes reduced throughput by 8–12% for balanced runs.
  - Pinning RDMA/NIC interfaces reduced cross-host variability but didn't fix intrahost NVSwitch queueing.
- Interpretation: no single static NCCL algorithm is best. Adaptive selection by the runtime based on observed link utilization might help.

Experiment 3: micro-batching and chunk sizing sweep
- Procedure: probe chunk sizes for weight sharding and tensor chunking (4MB -> 256KB). Observe NCCL op sizes and frequency.
- Observations:
  - Smaller chunk sizes increase NCCL op count but reduce single-op completion time; this attenuated tail in some skewed cases by allowing faster stragglers to catch up incrementally.
  - Too-small chunks increase CPU overhead and host staging bursts; identified sweet spot around 512KB–1MB for our model/config.

Experiment 4: asynchronous-replica-progressive-commit (proof-of-concept)
- Idea: let replicas proceed a bounded number of micro-steps ahead and use an opportunistic small cross-replica reconciliation window to reduce head-of-line blocking.
- Procedure: implemented a simple POC where if a replica is delayed by >X ms, other replicas continue for up to N micro-steps and attach a catch-up token for later merging. This was done only for non-critical allreduce ops (KV-cache replay metadata) in POC harness.
- Observations:
  - Reduced p99 by ~20–35% in tests with mild skew. For severe skew this introduced correctness complexity and occasional stalling during the reconciliation phase.
  - Good candidate for KV-cache and prefix-only operations where perfect sync is not required. Not safe for weight-sync reduction ops.

Production incident reproduction attempt (case INF-221):
- Incident: p99 tail-latency bursts for a hosted model after a customer launched a campaign. Hypothesized KV-cache replay storm to a small subset of replicas.
- Reproduction: replayed campaign trace; reproduced p99 spikes matching production telemetry. The replica skew aligned with a frequent prefix that hit local cache eviction pattern.
- Mitigation applied in-prod: rate-limited incoming requests per replica (soft throttle) + forced NCCL_TREE for group while we rotated kernels. Immediately reduced p99 by ~60%.

Key takeaways / heuristics:
- Replica skew amplifies interconnect-sensitive failure modes: NVSwitch link saturation, PCIe host-staging spikes, and NCCL collective stalls.
- Static NCCL algorithm choice is a poor long-term strategy; runtime should select algorithm based on (a) measured per-link queuing, (b) op size distribution, and (c) cross-replica variance.
- Chunk sizing for TP shards has a measurable effect on tail: use adaptive chunking that increases chunk count when link latency variability is high, but cap to avoid CPU/host pressure.
- Opportunistic asynchronous micro-progress helps for non-critical sync burdens (cache, metadata) but must be gated and auditable.

Short list of candidate runtime changes (prioritized):
1) telemetry-driven NCCL policy: probe link utilization and pick algorithm per-alloc group. Low-risk: start with canary per-cluster; metric: p99 reduction without throughput regression.
2) replica-aware request steering: simple first step => soft per-replica concurrency caps + global load shed for replicas crossing threshold. Medium risk operationally but easy rollback.
3) adaptive chunker: adjust TP chunk size at shard start based on recent link latency variance. Low-medium risk.
4) micro-async for cache ops: feature flag gated, only for KV/cache metadata. High risk for correctness; short-term POC only.
5) NVSwitch-aware placement: shift hot replicas to top-of-rack placements with fewer hop distances when possible. High operational complexity.

Quick reproducible commands / harness notes:
- run nccl-tests to get baseline per-link bandwidth: ./build/all_reduce_perf -b 8 -e 512M -f 2 -g 8
- nvlink perf telemetry: nvlinkperf --device=0 --duration=60
- capturing NCCL traces: export NCCL_DEBUG=INFO; run harness; collect logs under /var/log/serve-profiling/<run-id>
- to force algorithm: export NCCL_ALGO=Tree; export NCCL_MIN_NRINGS=1
- harness branch: GH-PR-3210 has the stress injectors and extra metrics endpoints (prom compatible).

Open questions and follow-ups:
- How to compute safe bounds for micro-async without risking model divergence? Need a small formal spec and unit tests against model outputs.
- Can we instrument NCCL or driver to get per-op queue depth for NVSwitch links? Current telemetry is coarse. Follow up with infra team (ticket JIRA-INF-842).
- Evaluate interplay with quantized kernels where compute time is shorter — does that hide or amplify interconnect imbalance?
- Explore per-replica token bucket rate-limiting in the gateway to avoid producer storms.

Next steps (owners + timeline):
- (Ethan) Implement telemetry-driven NCCL selector POC, due 2025-02-07.
- (Maya) Build per-replica soft concurrency limiter in the gateway, due 2025-01-31.
- (Liam) Add micro-chunk auto-tuner testbed and run sweep across model sizes, due 2025-02-14.
- (Zhang) Work with infra on finer-grained NVSwitch telemetry, escalate JIRA-INF-842, due 2025-02-21.

- Consider exposing an LED/metric in Console called "replica-skew-index" (0..1) for operations to quickly see skew across replica groups.
- For Dedicated customers consider an optional "balanced-arena" placement policy (replica placement affinity) to avoid hot-spotting across NVSwitch boundaries.

- 2024-11-08T15:32:01Z replica-2: kv-replay=214rps host-staging=1.8GB/s nvlink-qlen=12 allreduce-ms=32
- 2024-11-08T15:32:04Z replica-1: kv-replay=12rps host-staging=0.2GB/s nvlink-qlen=2 allreduce-ms=28
- 2024-11-08T15:32:20Z NCCL: op(allreduce) size=4MB duration=13ms participants=8 algo=ring

source.title: preemption-timeline-scratchpad
source.content:
Quick goal: capture a concise event timeline + commands tried + hypotheses for the Dedicated GPU pool preemption/eviction spike on 2025-01-12. This is a scratchpad — messy.

Symptoms observed:
- Sudden backlog of pending inference requests; tail latency jumped x10 for ~18m.
- Dedicated GPU pool showed repeated node add/remove cycles (~6 scale events in 20m).
- Several pods saw PodEvicted / NodeLost and FailedScheduling with 'insufficient nvidia.com/gpu' messages.
- Customer reported 502s around the same time (correlated to pool id: dedicated-eu-west-2-a).

Timeline (local times UTC):
- 2025-01-12 03:10: metric alert: "dedicated-eu-west-2-a.available_gpus" dropped below 4 (alert rule id 231).
- 03:11: autoscaler scale-up triggered (requested +2 nodes).
- 03:12: new nodes registered but many pods Pending due to initcontainers/driver probe flapping.
- 03:17: node kernel logs show multiple nvidia driver restarts (see kernel snippets below).
- 03:23: autoscaler marked nodes for scale-down (scale-down cooldown not respected?) and started terminating 2 nodes.
- 03:26: PodEvicted events increase; scheduler retries, some pods get scheduled on newly-created nodes at ~03:30.
- 03:35: backlog cleared. Incident closed in monitoring 03:38.

Commands / queries I ran (copy-and-paste friendly):
- kubectl get pods -A --field-selector=status.phase!=Running -o wide | grep dedicated-eu-west-2-a
- kubectl describe node ip-10-0-23-45 | sed -n '1,200p'  # look for Kubelet, driver, eviction messages
- kubectl logs -n kube-system nvidia-device-plugin-abc -c plugin | grep -i err -n || true
- gcloud compute instances describe ip-10-0-23-45 --zone=europe-west2-a --format='json' > node-describe.json
- prometheus query: increase(node_gpu_driver_restarts_total[5m]) by (instance)
- curl -s 'http://autoscaler.internal.cluster/scale/status?pool=dedicated-eu-west-2-a' | jq .

Interesting error snippets (log extracts):
- "kubelet eviction manager: eviction threshold breached: nodefs.available"  # correlated but not root cause
- "nvidia: Registered GPU 0 but driver failed to initialise (errno -1)"
- "DevicePlugin: failed to register device plugin: already registered for node"  # race? plugin restart loop
- "FailedScheduling: 0/12 nodes are available: 12 Insufficient nvidia.com/gpu."
- kubelet: "pod XYZ status: NodeLost - runtime network not ready"

First-pass hypotheses (ranked):
1) Driver/firmware instability on a subset of node types triggered repeated device-plugin restarts. New nodes come up but GPUs not ready -> scheduler sees "insufficient gpu" -> autoscaler keeps adding nodes because available pods still pending -> scale-down later removes nodes that had lingering pods -> thrash.
2) Autoscaler scale-down logic too aggressive given warm-start time of GPU nodes (GPU readiness ~4-6m). Scale-down cooldown or unbind policy misconfigured so nodes are removed before pods actually start accepting traffic.
3) Underlying host preemption or spot eviction spikes (cloud provider) caused nodes to disappear; cluster-autoscaler interpreted resource shortage and started adding more, leading to a wavey capacity curve.
4) A pod-level initcontainer is pinning GPUs (placeholder init step) and failing transiently, preventing normal scheduling and making scheduler appear starving for resources.

Experiments / checks to run next: (quick list)
- Reproduce locally on a small test pool with same image/driver combo: spin up 3 nodes, run stress-test to see driver restart behavior.
- Add a Prometheus alert for device-plugin restarts > 3 in 5m on a node (to detect pattern earlier).
- Confirm autoscaler settings: scale-down unneeded time, cooldown, max surge. Commands: kubectl -n kube-system get configmap cluster-autoscaler -o yaml | yq .data
- Inspect kernel logs around 03:12: journalctl -k --since '2025-01-12 03:00' -u kubelet | grep -i nvidia
- Capture full device plugin logs for nodes that flapped (kubectl logs node-agent -n kube-system --selector=node=ip-10-0-23-45)

Mitigations to try (short-term):
- Temporarily increase scale-down delay to 15m for dedicated pools. Change CS config: scaleDownUnneededTime to 15m for pool dedicated-eu-west-2-a.
- Add a readiness gate for GPU-heavy pods that waits for "nvidia.com/gpu-ready=true" annotation (set by node-init job). This prevents scheduling until GPU drivers are fully ready and might stop autoscaler from reacting prematurely.
- Triage node image -> roll a version with updated nvidia driver (if kernel logs show driver crash signatures matching known bug).

Notes-to-self / oddities:
- The device plugin reported "already registered" during the window — that suggests multiple plugin restarts overlapping with kubelet restarts. Could be race in init ordering (systemd unit ordering?).
- A handful of nodes show very high temperature readings before restart in telemetry (possible hardware/thermal issue). Cross-check with hardware team?
- I saw one instance where the autoscaler API returned 'scaleInCandidates' that included nodes with Pods in Terminating state for >2m. Why weren't those filtered?

Follow-ups (assign):
- @Maya: pull full kernel dmesg + driver versions for the 4 flapping nodes. (linked ticket INF-4721)
- @Priya: check cloud provider spot-eviction logs for the same timestamps and confirm if instance termination reason == 'preemptible' or 'maintenance'.
- @infra: consider short patch to increase scaleDownUnneededTime for dedicated pools until root cause identified.

Random commands/notes I saved for convenience:
- kubectl get events --all-namespaces --field-selector lastTimestamp>2025-01-12T03:00:00Z | grep -i Evicted || true
- prometheus: sum by(instance) (increase(nvidia_gpu_driver_restart_total[10m])) > 0
- grep -R "Device plugin" /var/log/containers/*nvidia* -n || true

If we want a short write-up for CS/ops: keep it simple: 'GPU driver instability caused device-plugin restarts which left new nodes in a not-ready-for-GPU state. Autoscaler added capacity to chase pending pods, then scale-down removed some nodes before pods were healthy, creating thrash and a latency spike.'

Last random thought: check whether our node image includes the new persistent device-plugin that supports preStart hooks — that would let us mark node as ready only after driver probe passes. If not, consider upstreaming a tiny readiness wrapper.

End scratchpad.

title: preemption-timeline-scratchpad
preview:
Quick goal: capture a concise event timeline + commands tried + hypotheses for the Dedicated GPU pool preemption/eviction spike on 2025-01-12. This is a scratchpad — messy.

Symptoms observed:
- Sudden backlog of pending inference requests; tail latency jumped x10 for ~18m.
- Dedicated GPU pool showed repeated node add/remove cycles (~6 scale events in 20m).
- Several pods saw PodEvicted / NodeLost and FailedScheduling with 'insufficient nvidia.com/gpu' messages.
- Customer reported 502s around the same time (correlated to pool id: dedicated-eu-west-2-a).

Timeline (local times UTC):
- 2025-01-12 03:10: metric alert: "dedicated-eu-west-2-a.available_gpus" dropped below 4 (alert rule id 231).
- 03:11: autoscaler scale-up triggered (requested +2 nodes).
- 03:12: new nodes registered but many pods Pending due to initcontainers/driver probe flapping.
- 03:17: node kernel logs show multiple nvidia driver restarts (see kernel snippets below).
- 03:23: autoscaler marked nodes for scale-down (scale-down cooldown not respected?) and started terminating 2 nodes.
- 03:26: PodEvicted events increase; scheduler retries, some pods get scheduled on newly-created nodes at ~03:30.
- 03:35: backlog cleared. Incident closed in monitoring 03:38.

Commands / queries I ran (copy-and-paste friendly):
- kubectl get pods -A --field-selector=status.phase!=Running -o wide | grep dedicated-eu-west-2-a
- kubectl describe node ip-10-0-23-45 | sed -n '1,200p'  # look for Kubelet, driver, eviction messages
- kubectl logs -n kube-system nvidia-device-plugin-abc -c plugin | grep -i err -n || true
- gcloud compute instances describe ip-10-0-23-45 --zone=europe-west2-a --format='json' > node-describe.json
- prometheus query: increase(node_gpu_driver_restarts_total[5m]) by (instance)
- curl -s 'http://autoscaler.internal.cluster/scale/status?pool=dedicated-eu-west-2-a' | jq .

Interesting error snippets (log extracts):
- "kubelet eviction manager: eviction threshold breached: nodefs.available"  # correlated but not root cause
- "nvidia: Registered GPU 0 but driver failed to initialise (errno -1)"
- "DevicePlugin: failed to register device plugin: already registered for node"  # race? plugin restart loop
- "FailedScheduling: 0/12 nodes are available: 12 Insufficient nvidia.com/gpu."
- kubelet: "pod XYZ status: NodeLost - runtime network not ready"

First-pass hypotheses (ranked):
1) Driver/firmware instability on a subset of node types triggered repeated device-plugin restarts. New nodes come up but GPUs not ready -> scheduler sees "insufficient gpu" -> autoscaler keeps adding nodes because available pods still pending -> scale-down later removes nodes that had lingering pods -> thrash.
2) Autoscaler scale-down logic too aggressive given warm-start time of GPU nodes (GPU readiness ~4-6m). Scale-down cooldown or unbind policy misconfigured so nodes are removed before pods actually start accepting traffic.
3) Underlying host preemption or spot eviction spikes (cloud provider) caused nodes to disappear; cluster-autoscaler interpreted resource shortage and started adding more, leading to a wavey capacity curve.
4) A pod-level initcontainer is pinning GPUs (placeholder init step) and failing transiently, preventing normal scheduling and making scheduler appear starving for resources.

Experiments / checks to run next: (quick list)
- Reproduce locally on a small test pool with same image/driver combo: spin up 3 nodes, run stress-test to see driver restart behavior.
- Add a Prometheus alert for device-plugin restarts > 3 in 5m on a node (to detect pattern earlier).
- Confirm autoscaler settings: scale-down unneeded time, cooldown, max surge. Commands: kubectl -n kube-system get configmap cluster-autoscaler -o yaml | yq .data
- Inspect kernel logs around 03:12: journalctl -k --since '2025-01-12 03:00' -u kubelet | grep -i nvidia
- Capture full device plugin logs for nodes that flapped (kubectl logs node-agent -n kube-system --selector=node=ip-10-0-23-45)

Mitigations to try (short-term):
- Temporarily increase scale-down delay to 15m for dedicated pools. Change CS config: scaleDownUnneededTime to 15m for pool dedicated-eu-west-2-a.
- Add a readiness gate for GPU-heavy pods that waits for "nvidia.com/gpu-ready=true" annotation (set by node-init job). This prevents scheduling until GPU drivers are fully ready and might stop autoscaler from reacting prematurely.
- Triage node image -> roll a version with updated nvidia driver (if kernel logs show driver crash signatures matching known bug).

Notes-to-self / oddities:
- The device plugin reported "already registered" during the window — that suggests multiple plugin restarts overlapping with kubelet restarts. Could be race in init ordering (systemd unit ordering?).
- A handful of nodes show very high temperature readings before restart in telemetry (possible hardware/thermal issue). Cross-check with hardware team?
- I saw one instance where the autoscaler API returned 'scaleInCandidates' that included nodes with Pods in Terminating state for >2m. Why weren't those filtered?

Follow-ups (assign):
- @Maya: pull full kernel dmesg + driver versions for the 4 flapping nodes. (linked ticket INF-4721)
- @Priya: check cloud provider spot-eviction logs for the same timestamps and confirm if instance termination reason == 'preemptible' or 'maintenance'.
- @infra: consider short patch to increase scaleDownUnneededTime for dedicated pools until root cause identified.

Random commands/notes I saved for convenience:
- kubectl get events --all-namespaces --field-selector lastTimestamp>2025-01-12T03:00:00Z | grep -i Evicted || true
- prometheus: sum by(instance) (increase(nvidia_gpu_driver_restart_total[10m])) > 0
- grep -R "Device plugin" /var/log/containers/*nvidia* -n || true

If we want a short write-up for CS/ops: keep it simple: 'GPU driver instability caused device-plugin restarts which left new nodes in a not-ready-for-GPU state. Autoscaler added capacity to chase pending pods, then scale-down removed some nodes before pods were healthy, creating thrash and a latency spike.'

Last random thought: check whether our node image includes the new persistent device-plugin that supports preStart hooks — that would let us mark node as ready only after driver probe passes. If not, consider upstreaming a tiny readiness wrapper.

End scratchpad.

source.title: portable-eval-harness-cli-and-manifest-scratchpad
source.content:
Purpose
-----
Notes for a small, portable evaluation harness that devs can run locally or in CI to validate model behavior (generation, chat, structure, embeddings). Focus: reproducible runs, simple manifest, CLI ergonomics, and a lightweight golden-output ledger that can be versioned alongside code.

Motivation / problem statements
- We run large eval suites in infra but small quick checks are painful to author/run locally.
- Need a single manifest format that describes input prompts, expected outputs (optional), tolerances, metric hooks, and seed/runtime constraints.
- CLI should make it trivial to: run single-case, run a manifest, diff outputs vs golden, collect metrics, and emit machine-readable reports for CI.

Principles
- Lightweight: no heavy infra required to start. Single binary (py CLI or go) with pluggable backend adapters.
- Deterministic where possible: explicit seeds, tokenizer/version pinned, model variant id.
- Portable: manifest + golden outputs can live in repo, supports running offline with local model runtime.
- Auditable: each run writes a run-metadata header linking to commit, manifest id, CLI version, random seed.

Primary user flows
1) Local dev smoke: run one case to inspect generation and tweak prompts.
   $ rdweval run --case prompt-001.yaml --model local:gptq-8bit --seed 42 --out out.json

2) Manifest run: run whole folder or manifest; produce summary and per-case JSONL.
   $ rdweval run --manifest evals/checkout-flow.yaml --concurrency 4 --report report.json

3) Diff vs golden (CI gating): compute pass/fail and upload artifact.
   $ rdweval diff --manifest evals/checkout-flow.yaml --golden-dir evals/golden --thresholds thresholds.yaml

4) Create/update golden interactively (dev-controlled): open diffs and accept changes.
   $ rdweval review --case checkout-03 --golden-dir evals/golden

Manifest schema (sketch)
- short human-readable YAML that maps to an ordered list of cases. Example below. Keep it minimal so writing by hand is easy.

--- manifest id: checkout-flow
cases:
  - id: checkout-01
    tags: [sanity, low-latency]
    input:
      type: chat
      messages:
        - role: system
          content: "You are a helpful checkout assistant."
        - role: user
          content: "I want to buy a pair of shoes. What info do you need?"
    model: "open:rwkv-small-v2"
    constraints:
      max_tokens: 128
      temperature: 0.0
      seed: 12345
    validations:
      - kind: contains
        path: text
        expect: "shipping address"
      - kind: regex
        path: text
        pattern: "(order|checkout)"
  - id: checkout-02
    input: { type: text, prompt: "Summarize: long user message..." }
    model: "local:gptq-8bit"
    constraints: { temperature: 0.2, max_tokens: 60 }

Notes on manifest fields
- "model" is a runtime-agnostic shorthand: <flavor>:<id> where flavor can be local, hosted, sim, float32 etc. CLI resolves to adapter.
- "validations" are small unit checks (contains, equals, regex, semantic-similarity). Each validation can declare a tolerance: e.g., similarity >= 0.87.
- Allow arbitrary metadata for grouping, e.g., tags and owner for triage.

Golden ledger format (simple)
- Per-manifest golden directory: evals/golden/<manifest-id>/case-id.json
- Each golden file is a JSON with flat keys: text, tokens, tokenizer_version, model_revision, created_by, created_at, manifest_id, case_id, source_commit, notes.
- When running diff, the CLI compares validations first and then falls back to fuzzy text diff (Levenshtein / normalized BLEU / embedding similarity) if no direct validation.

Run metadata header (always output at top of JSON)
- rdweval_version: string
- cli_args: string
- run_id: uuid
- commit: git sha
- started_at, finished_at
- env: { model_backend: string, gpu: bool }  <-- we will flatten this in artifacts but keep keys readable.

Reproducibility knobs
- seed: explicit numeric seed used for RNGs. If missing, CLI prints a generated seed and stores it in outputs.
- tokenizer: pinned tokenizer version/hash; if tokenizer is out of tree, CLI saves tokenizer fingerprint.
- deterministic-mode flag: disable sampling ops that rely on nondeterminism. Useful for unit tests.

Output artifacts
- per-case JSONL entries (one line per case) with run metadata header repeated for provenance.
- report.json: summary of passes/fails, aggregate metrics (exact match rate, avg similarity, toxicity hits).
- diff bundles: tarball of case outputs and diffs for artifact upload in CI.

Metric hooks and labels
- Allow per-manifest metric hooks: functions that map case output to scalar. Examples: rouge, embedding-similarity, token-count. Hooks are small Python entrypoints or a WASM path.
- CLI loads hooks by name or path: --hook-path hooks/rouge.py

CI integration pattern
- CI job checks out repo, installs small runtime shim (rdw-eval-runner), runs rdweval diff against golden, fails job if any case fails validations.
- For approved golden updates, a separate job posts a PR with updated golden artifacts and human review flag.

Local dev UX ideas
- interactive mode: rdweval inspect --case checkout-01 pops an ncurses-ish viewer showing prompt, tokenization, model output, and quick accept/accept-and-update-golden.
- --replay flag to feed exact network responses or model outputs (to unit test orchestration without hitting model infra).
- seed-sweeper: run same case across seeds to surface brittle prompts.

Security/PII considerations
- Manifest and golden files may contain user-like content. Provide a scrubber step: rdweval scrub --manifest X --rules rules.yaml that redacts emails, phone numbers before committing.
- Provide a whitelist for golden updates that require encryption or gated PR workflow for golden artifacts with sensitive content.

Prototype CLI layout (commands)
- rdweval init --manifest-name checkout-flow  # scaffolds a manifest and golden folder
- rdweval run [--manifest M | --case C] [--model M] [--out DIR] [--concurrency N] [--seed S]
- rdweval diff --manifest M --golden-dir G --thresholds T --report out.json
- rdweval review --case C --golden-dir G  # interactive accept/reject
- rdweval scrub --manifest M --rules R
- rdweval hook-validate --hook hooks/score.py --manifest M

Open questions / decisions to make
- Where should golden artifacts live by default? In-repo makes PR-based updates easy, but large binary artifacts (embeddings, many outputs) bloat repo. Maybe golden-text-only in repo + large bundles stored in s3/gs with manifest pointers.
- How strict should default validations be? Fail-fast for CI but allow "soft-fail" mode that emits warnings.
- Should the CLI enforce a manifest schema version and provide migration helpers? Probably yes.

Short TODOs (prioritized)
- [ ] Implement minimal CLI with run/diff/init (prototype in Python click)
- [ ] Add manifest parser + basic validations (contains/equals/regex)
- [ ] Define golden JSON shape and writer helper
- [ ] Build CI job example YAML and add to example repo under infra/ci/rdweval.yaml
- [ ] Implement scrubber with common PII rules (emails, phones, credit card-like patterns)
- [ ] Write short dev guide: README.md with examples and recommended workflow

Notes / links for follow-up
- Related GH PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/rdw-eval-runner/pull/142 (proto CLI + manifest parser)
- Slack thread: #eval-tooling (search: "golden ledger")
- Jira ticket: PROD-2411 (CI gate for model-sanity checks)

Random sketches / idea dump (brainstorm)
- "golden diffs as first-class review comments": small helper that posts case diffs as inline comments on PRs so reviewers can approve behavioral changes.
- automatic triage: when a diff fails, run a light-weight classifier to mark likely "regression" vs "expected drift" and tag owner.
- build a tiny web viewer (static) that can render report.json + diffs for reviewers without running anything.

End of scratchpad.

-- ethan

text:
Purpose
-----
Notes for a small, portable evaluation harness that devs can run locally or in CI to validate model behavior (generation, chat, structure, embeddings). Focus: reproducible runs, simple manifest, CLI ergonomics, and a lightweight golden-output ledger that can be versioned alongside code.

Motivation / problem statements
- We run large eval suites in infra but small quick checks are painful to author/run locally.
- Need a single manifest format that describes input prompts, expected outputs (optional), tolerances, metric hooks, and seed/runtime constraints.
- CLI should make it trivial to: run single-case, run a manifest, diff outputs vs golden, collect metrics, and emit machine-readable reports for CI.

Principles
- Lightweight: no heavy infra required to start. Single binary (py CLI or go) with pluggable backend adapters.
- Deterministic where possible: explicit seeds, tokenizer/version pinned, model variant id.
- Portable: manifest + golden outputs can live in repo, supports running offline with local model runtime.
- Auditable: each run writes a run-metadata header linking to commit, manifest id, CLI version, random seed.

Primary user flows
1) Local dev smoke: run one case to inspect generation and tweak prompts.
   $ rdweval run --case prompt-001.yaml --model local:gptq-8bit --seed 42 --out out.json

2) Manifest run: run whole folder or manifest; produce summary and per-case JSONL.
   $ rdweval run --manifest evals/checkout-flow.yaml --concurrency 4 --report report.json

3) Diff vs golden (CI gating): compute pass/fail and upload artifact.
   $ rdweval diff --manifest evals/checkout-flow.yaml --golden-dir evals/golden --thresholds thresholds.yaml

4) Create/update golden interactively (dev-controlled): open diffs and accept changes.
   $ rdweval review --case checkout-03 --golden-dir evals/golden

Manifest schema (sketch)
- short human-readable YAML that maps to an ordered list of cases. Example below. Keep it minimal so writing by hand is easy.

--- manifest id: checkout-flow
cases:
  - id: checkout-01
    tags: [sanity, low-latency]
    input:
      type: chat
      messages:
        - role: system
          content: "You are a helpful checkout assistant."
        - role: user
          content: "I want to buy a pair of shoes. What info do you need?"
    model: "open:rwkv-small-v2"
    constraints:
      max_tokens: 128
      temperature: 0.0
      seed: 12345
    validations:
      - kind: contains
        path: text
        expect: "shipping address"
      - kind: regex
        path: text
        pattern: "(order|checkout)"
  - id: checkout-02
    input: { type: text, prompt: "Summarize: long user message..." }
    model: "local:gptq-8bit"
    constraints: { temperature: 0.2, max_tokens: 60 }

Notes on manifest fields
- "model" is a runtime-agnostic shorthand: <flavor>:<id> where flavor can be local, hosted, sim, float32 etc. CLI resolves to adapter.
- "validations" are small unit checks (contains, equals, regex, semantic-similarity). Each validation can declare a tolerance: e.g., similarity >= 0.87.
- Allow arbitrary metadata for grouping, e.g., tags and owner for triage.

Golden ledger format (simple)
- Per-manifest golden directory: evals/golden/<manifest-id>/case-id.json
- Each golden file is a JSON with flat keys: text, tokens, tokenizer_version, model_revision, created_by, created_at, manifest_id, case_id, source_commit, notes.
- When running diff, the CLI compares validations first and then falls back to fuzzy text diff (Levenshtein / normalized BLEU / embedding similarity) if no direct validation.

Run metadata header (always output at top of JSON)
- rdweval_version: string
- cli_args: string
- run_id: uuid
- commit: git sha
- started_at, finished_at
- env: { model_backend: string, gpu: bool }  <-- we will flatten this in artifacts but keep keys readable.

Reproducibility knobs
- seed: explicit numeric seed used for RNGs. If missing, CLI prints a generated seed and stores it in outputs.
- tokenizer: pinned tokenizer version/hash; if tokenizer is out of tree, CLI saves tokenizer fingerprint.
- deterministic-mode flag: disable sampling ops that rely on nondeterminism. Useful for unit tests.

Output artifacts
- per-case JSONL entries (one line per case) with run metadata header repeated for provenance.
- report.json: summary of passes/fails, aggregate metrics (exact match rate, avg similarity, toxicity hits).
- diff bundles: tarball of case outputs and diffs for artifact upload in CI.

Metric hooks and labels
- Allow per-manifest metric hooks: functions that map case output to scalar. Examples: rouge, embedding-similarity, token-count. Hooks are small Python entrypoints or a WASM path.
- CLI loads hooks by name or path: --hook-path hooks/rouge.py

CI integration pattern
- CI job checks out repo, installs small runtime shim (rdw-eval-runner), runs rdweval diff against golden, fails job if any case fails validations.
- For approved golden updates, a separate job posts a PR with updated golden artifacts and human review flag.

Local dev UX ideas
- interactive mode: rdweval inspect --case checkout-01 pops an ncurses-ish viewer showing prompt, tokenization, model output, and quick accept/accept-and-update-golden.
- --replay flag to feed exact network responses or model outputs (to unit test orchestration without hitting model infra).
- seed-sweeper: run same case across seeds to surface brittle prompts.

Security/PII considerations
- Manifest and golden files may contain user-like content. Provide a scrubber step: rdweval scrub --manifest X --rules rules.yaml that redacts emails, phone numbers before committing.
- Provide a whitelist for golden updates that require encryption or gated PR workflow for golden artifacts with sensitive content.

Prototype CLI layout (commands)
- rdweval init --manifest-name checkout-flow  # scaffolds a manifest and golden folder
- rdweval run [--manifest M | --case C] [--model M] [--out DIR] [--concurrency N] [--seed S]
- rdweval diff --manifest M --golden-dir G --thresholds T --report out.json
- rdweval review --case C --golden-dir G  # interactive accept/reject
- rdweval scrub --manifest M --rules R
- rdweval hook-validate --hook hooks/score.py --manifest M

Open questions / decisions to make
- Where should golden artifacts live by default? In-repo makes PR-based updates easy, but large binary artifacts (embeddings, many outputs) bloat repo. Maybe golden-text-only in repo + large bundles stored in s3/gs with manifest pointers.
- How strict should default validations be? Fail-fast for CI but allow "soft-fail" mode that emits warnings.
- Should the CLI enforce a manifest schema version and provide migration helpers? Probably yes.

Short TODOs (prioritized)
- [ ] Implement minimal CLI with run/diff/init (prototype in Python click)
- [ ] Add manifest parser + basic validations (contains/equals/regex)
- [ ] Define golden JSON shape and writer helper
- [ ] Build CI job example YAML and add to example repo under infra/ci/rdweval.yaml
- [ ] Implement scrubber with common PII rules (emails, phones, credit card-like patterns)
- [ ] Write short dev guide: README.md with examples and recommended workflow

Notes / links for follow-up
- Related GH PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/rdw-eval-runner/pull/142 (proto CLI + manifest parser)
- Slack thread: #eval-tooling (search: "golden ledger")
- Jira ticket: PROD-2411 (CI gate for model-sanity checks)

Random sketches / idea dump (brainstorm)
- "golden diffs as first-class review comments": small helper that posts case diffs as inline comments on PRs so reviewers can approve behavioral changes.
- automatic triage: when a diff fails, run a light-weight classifier to mark likely "regression" vs "expected drift" and tag owner.
- build a tiny web viewer (static) that can render report.json + diffs for reviewers without running anything.

End of scratchpad.

-- ethan

source.title: Attention prefetch tolerance and cost-model notes
source.content:
Summary:
This is a working note exploring the notion of a "prefetch tolerance" — the window (in tokens / microseconds) where aggressive KV/prefix prefetching improves tail latency and unit-cost, versus when it increases register pressure, causes bank conflicts, or forces kernel deoptimizations (paged -> flash fallbacks) that hurt throughput. The goal is to capture qualitative heuristics and a simple cost model we can use in the scheduler to decide prefetch aggressiveness per request/queue.

Why this matters:
- We see cases where enabling prefix/KV prefetching for medium-length chats reduces average latency but increases 95/99p tail because fusedops run with higher register pressure and start evicting tiled scratchpad pages.
- Autoscaling and routing decisions depend on predicted cost per token; prefetching knobs can swing that by 5–30% depending on workload mix.
- Current heuristics are binary (prefetch=on/off) in a few codepaths; want a graded control and conservative thresholds.

Definitions / signals to use:
- prefetch_window_tokens: how many prior tokens to include when issuing prefetch (0 = no prefetch).
- prefetch_issued_latency: time taken to enqueue prefetch ops (ms).
- kernel_variant: {flash, paged, fusedops, scalar-fallback} selected for execution after scheduling.
- register_pressure_index (RPI): empirical proxy from kernel telemetry (0..1); values >0.7 correlate with fusion instability in current kernels.
- eviction_fraction: fraction of scratchpad pages evicted during run (observed from device telemetry).

Experiment matrix (what we ran):
- Model family: distilled-gpt-like (base attention), and a 2.7B dense decoder (representative compute patterns).
- Hardware: A100-40GB, driver 525.xx, cuda 11.x.
- Sequence mixes: {short bias: 1–64 tokens}, {mixed: 8/128/512}, {long bias: 256–1024}.
- Batch sizes: small interactive (1–4), medium (8–16), large (32+ coalesced).
- Prefetch windows tested: 0, 8, 32, 128, full-prefix.
- Kernel builds: flashattention v2 (fused-kernel enabled), paged-attn (flash-paged), and a debug build with aggressive register logging.

High level observations:
1) Short-interactive workloads (1–64 tokens): prefetch_window 8–32 reduced mean latency by ~6–12% and slightly improved requester-side CPU wait. Tail (99p) sometimes increased when RPI>0.72 because fusedops fell back mid-kernel; impact visible as 3–8% latency spikes.
2) Mixed workloads: a single prefetch policy across a queue performed poorly: small requests benefited while large requests degraded. Mixed queues amplify kernel-switching overhead (overhead ~0.8–1.4 ms per switch) and raised CPU scheduling jitter.
3) Long sequences: prefetching larger windows helped throughput up to a point (window ~128 tokens), but full-prefix prefetch caused memory thrashing on paged-attn and increased OOM rate in stress tests.
4) Fusion sensitivity: fusedops are the most sensitive to prefetch-induced register pressure. When register_pressure_index > 0.7, even modest prefetch windows caused fusedops to reorder into less efficient kernels or trigger small scalar fallbacks.

Quantitative snippets (example run, mixed workload):
- baseline (prefetch=0, batch=8, mixed seq): p50=12.3ms p95=27.9ms p99=45.6ms; estimated token-cost 1.00x
- prefetch=32: p50=11.2ms p95=28.8ms p99=52.4ms; cost estimate 0.94x (more kernel cost + higher retries)
- prefetch=128: p50=10.6ms p95=34.1ms p99=78.5ms; cost estimate 1.12x (throttling + fallback kernel overhead)
(These numbers are illustrative from runs on 2024-10-27 harness; absolute numbers vary by driver and host load.)

Cost-model sketch (operational):
We want a cheap runtime estimator that takes request metadata (seq_len, expected continuation length, priority, batch_coalescing_probability) and returns a prefetch_budget (tokens) and conservative flag. Basic form:
cost_est = alpha * marginal_token_time(prefetch=0) + beta * addl_prefetch_overhead(prefetch_window) + gamma * fallback_risk(prefetch_window, RPI_est)
where fallback_risk is approximated from recent telemetry: fallback_risk = sigmoid(k * (RPI_recent - rpi_thresh)) and RPI_recent is a sliding fraction of runs with RPI>0.7 in the last N minutes.
Decision rule examples:
- if expected_continuation < 16 and latency_slo tight -> prefetch_window = min(8, remaining_prefix)
- if queue_mixture_entropy high or RPI_recent > 0.6 -> prefetch_window = 0 (favor stability)
- for dedicated low-variance lanes (committed capacity) -> allow prefetch_window up to 128 with monitoring and auto-throttle on RPI spikes

Scheduler / implementation ideas:
- per-route prefetch_profile: {aggressiveness: low/med/high, rpi_sensitivity: float, max_window: int}
- lightweight RPI telemetry aggregated per-GPU and per-model; scheduler consults a short-timescale RPI_est and reduces aggressiveness if above thresholds.
- optimistic prefetch: issue but set early-cancel point if kernels show high register pressure; requires improved kernel cancelation hooks (TODO: infra change).
- bump heuristics: favor prefetch for requests that are likely to be batched with similar seq_len shortly (detect via route-level recent coalescing stats).

Open issues and unknowns:
- RPI estimation quality: current RPI proxies are noisy on machines with background work; need to validate whether per-stream RPI or per-GPU aggregation is better for decisions.
- Kernel-handshake cost variance: we observed handshake costs vary non-linearly with host SM clocks; need to correlate with thermal/power states.
- Interaction with quantized kernels: lower-precision fusedops have different register behavior; not fully explored.
- Paging thresholds: the point at which paged-attn starts thrashing depends heavily on page size and allocator fragmentation. We should capture fragmentation metrics in telemetry.

Action items (near-term):
- (Ethan) Hook a low-overhead RPI counter in the fusedop telemetry path and expose as per-GPU rolling metric. (due 2025-01-17)
- (Marcos) Add per-route prefetch_profile config to the scheduler API; default to conservative. (PR #842 reviewer)
- (Priya) Run cross-driver batch of mixed workload stress-tests to measure handshake variance across driver versions (due 2025-02-01).
- Add an alert that trips when per-node fallback_rate > 3% sustained for 5 minutes (SRE + infra).

Notes for reviewers:
- This is intentionally conservative: aim is to reduce surprising 99p regressions when we flip prefetch defaults.
- If we push aggressive defaults for Dedicated, pair with a canary circuit that automatically reduces aggressiveness on early fallback signs.

References and links:
- PR with initial telemetry hooks: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/842
- Related kernel-fusion notes (internal): kernel-fusion-boundary-investigation.json (see drive)
- Slack thread: slack:#attn-kernel (thread: tX3f9)

Questions for triage meeting:
- Do we want per-customer prefetch defaults exposed in Dedicated config? (ops cost vs control)
- Can we get a small kernel API change to allow prefetch cancellation if early signs of register pressure appear?
- How aggressive should the default be for hosted API routes vs internal dev/test routes?

Quick appendix (debugging checklist):
- capture: detailed fusedop register usage + warp occupancy metrics for any run with fallback
- reproduce: use the harness run-mix-mimic with identical batch/seq seed and driver pins
- mitigate: set prefetch_window=0 on offending route, reduce concurrency, enable fallback throttling

End of note.

- reproduce: use the harness run-mix-mimic with identical batch/seq seed and driver pins
- mitigate: set prefetch_window=0 on offending route, reduce concurrency, enable fallback throttling

End of note.

source.title: GPU pool resiliency & degenerate-burst handbook — wireframes + backlog
source.content:
Purpose
This is a working sketch + backlog for a new console surface focused on GPU pool resiliency and handling degenerate burst patterns. The intent is to reduce operator cognitive load when pools behave unexpectedly (spikes, noisy-tenants, slow-starting models), provide clear fallbacks, and make the tradeoffs of burst/reservation actions explicit.

Context and motivation
- We've got a class of incidents where small, frequent short-lived requests create lumpy KV-cache/truncation churn that starves long-running streams. Existing pool views show utilization and reservations but don't make "resiliency state" or fallback behaviors visible.
- Dev teams ask for a predictable way to: 1) isolate noisy tenants, 2) temporarily route low-priority traffic to lower-cost model variants, and 3) simulate burst scenarios to evaluate SLAs.
- This doc takes UX-first approach: wireframes for a Pool Resilience panel + a small Playbook modal + actionable backlog items to reach MVP.

Top-level wireframe description (canvas)
1) Header: Pool name, region, primary GPU type, last health check (green/yellow/red pill). Quick-action dropdown: "Force drain", "Start simulated burst", "Mark degraded".
2) Resiliency ribbon (prominent, under header): a compact set of chips showing resiliency signals: "prefix-cache-hit-rate", "KV-eviction-rate", "avg-latency-p50/p95", "preemption-events". Each chip is clickable and expands a 4-line mini-trend.
3) Live timeline: horizontally scrollable sparkline with three stacked series: active requests, queue length, eviction count. Hover shows exact counts and the top contributing job IDs (clickable to open job detail).
4) Pool card grid: each card = node group (e.g., gtx-80x-1.2). Card shows current kernels, seq-length heatmap, memory pressure gauge, and a small badge for "noisy tenant" if applicable. Cards have inline affordances for "take offline" and "cordon".
5) Panic controls (right-side): an accordion with prescriptive actions: "Scale burst" (temporary +overcommit), "Lower model quality", "Drain low-priority routes". Each control shows estimated latency/cost impact and a confidence bar (low/med/high).
6) Simulation playground: bottom of the page — small form to create synthetic burst scenarios (QPS, seq-length dist, prefix-reuse fraction). Submit triggers a sandboxed simulation run and writes a short report (3–5 sentences) about likely effects.

Modal: Playbook (on incident open)
- When an SRE clicks "Open playbook", modal shows: incident summary (auto-populated from last 15m), suggested immediate steps (safe order), and a one-click action to apply a rollback policy (re-route to fallback model variant). Includes "Why this step" short rationale.
- Micro-copy reminding about cost / customer impact for each action. Eg: "Scaling burst +50% may reduce latency but increase cost by ~2.3x for the window."

Micro-interactions and error states
- Try action -> show dry-run preview (expected token cost delta + latency delta). Confirm action -> optimistic UI + toast, but also show a cancellable revert for 60s.
- If a control fails (e.g., reservation API 503), show inline diagnostics with the raw error and a link to the runtime logs (small expander).

Accessibility and telemetry hooks
- Keyboard nav for primary controls; color contrast for resiliency chips; ARIA labels for "take offline" and "simulate burst".
- Events to send: action_queued, action_applied, simulation_started, simulation_completed (with summary metrics). Include correlation id.

Edge cases to call out (UX-specific)
- Degenerate bursts where lots of small warmup requests fill KV cache and cause eviction thrashing. UI should surface "KV churn" as a first-class signal and show recent top prefixes.
- Noisy-tenant detection needs to be fuzzy: combine qps/spike-frequency and low-cache-hit ratio. Present as "probable noisy-tenant" with confidence band.
- When pool is multi-tenant and a reservation overlaps an in-progress canary, present a conflict dialog that shows affected routes and suggested resolution (e.g., move canary to a dedicated reserved slot).

Backlog (MVP -> next iterations)
- MVP 1: Resiliency ribbon + basic live timeline + pool cards (P0, 2 sprint): acceptance: ribbon shows three metrics, timeline updates at 5s, can click node card to cordon. Linked artifacts: INF-2894.
- MVP 2: Playbook modal + panic controls (P0, 1 sprint): acceptance: modal shows suggested steps and performs one-click re-route to fallback model variant in sandbox. UX copy reviewed by product.
- MVP 3: Simulation playground + dry-run estimates (P1, 2 sprints): acceptance: run simulation returns expected impacts and writes a short simulation report.
- P1: Noisy-tenant tagging and inline eviction logs (1 sprint): acceptance: UI surfaces top 3 suspected tenants with quick links to their reservations.
- P2: Cost-estimator integration (estimate dollars per burst) + audit trail export (1 sprint).
- Stretch: "Auto-heal" policies that can be toggled per-pool (policy builder UX) + A/B rollout controls for fallback variants.

Draft acceptance criteria examples (copy these into tickets)
- Timeline updates at least every 5 seconds under 2k QPS; hovering shows data for the last 60 minutes.
- Playbook actions must record an audit entry with actor, timestamp, pre/post state.
- Simulation run must be non-destructive (sandboxed) and must not change production routing unless explicitly confirmed.

Open questions / decisions
- How to compute and display "confidence" for noisy-tenant detection? Thresholds in infra or heuristic model in platform? (SRE vs Applied ML question)
- Should "lower model quality" be implemented as a separate route in routing layer or as an inline real-time model-swap? (Impacts rollout complexity)
- Where should cost-estimates come from — historical token-costs or static unit prices? Mixed approach might be necessary.

Next steps / meeting agenda (for sync with SRE + Applied ML)
- 2024-11-18 design review: walk wireframes, pick MVP scope.
- 2024-11-22 infra spike: feasibility of live KV churn metric (owner: Liam).
- 2024-11-29 proto demo: simulation playground basic output (owner: Maya).

Notes to reviewers (informal)
- This is intentionally a working doc — expect shorthand, rough acceptance criteria, and TODO bullets. Please leave inline comments on the specific wireframe sections you think are risky.
- Priya: can you sanity-check the playbook copy for legal/regulatory language re: customer-impact notices?

Quick TODOs (owner next steps)
- Ethan: mock up 2 alternative resiliency ribbon layouts (compact vs detailed) by 2024-11-15.
- Sofia: draft the audit-event schema for playbook actions (due 2024-11-20).
- Liam: prototype KV churn metric and a simple noisy-tenant detector (spike detector) by 2024-11-22.

source.title: Adaptive delta slotting and epochal hinting experiments
source.content:
Summary

This doc captures a set of small-scale experiments exploring two complementary techniques to reduce KV cache fragmentation and lower tail latency under mixed workloads: (1) adaptive delta slotting — a dynamic variable-slot allocator that adjusts slot grain on per-key-size distributions, and (2) epochal hinting — a lightweight metadata epoch per request that biases retention/eviction without heavy global counters. The goal: materially reduce memory waste (measured as unusable internal fragmentation) while keeping eviction cost and allocation latency low enough for 99.9% SLOs.

Background and motivation

We’ve been seeing two recurring issues: (a) high internal fragmentation from uniform slot sizes when serving a mix of short and long KV blobs (e.g., chat contexts vs. embeddings), and (b) eviction thrash when tenant request patterns change rapidly (bursty prefix access). Existing slab-like pools reduce external fragmentation but still leave many partially-used slots across tenants. Full compaction and stop-the-world GC are expensive. We need incremental, low-latency techniques that adapt to workload skew and temporal locality.

High-level approach

1) Adaptive delta slotting (ADS)
- Idea: maintain multiple slot "deltas" per shard where a delta is a small set of slot size classes but the mapping of keys -> class is probabilistic and adjustable. Instead of fixed slab bins (16B, 32B, 64B, ...), ADS tracks an empirical size histogram per shard and then selects a compact set of bucket widths that minimize worst-case internal waste under a constrained number of classes (e.g., 6 classes).
- Runtime behavior: on allocation, probe active delta classes; if none fit the request cheaply, ADS will (a) fast-path split a larger free slot (if available), or (b) fallback to an overflow compacting allocation (rare). A background thread recomputes the delta set every epoch window (configurable, 1–10s) using a lightweight quantile approximation (t-digest variant) to avoid locking hot paths.
- Goals: reduce average unused bytes-per-entry and reduce cross-tenant interferences by making slot classes match observed workload. Lower-cost in-place split/merge operations instead of heavy relocation.

2) Epochal hinting (EH)
- Idea: attach a tiny epoch id (8-12 bits) to requests served by a worker; when a key is referenced, its metadata stores the current epoch tag. Eviction/retention heuristics use epoch-tag recency and cross-epoch hit counts to estimate short-term hotness without maintaining heavy LRU lists or global timestamps.
- Mechanics: serve loop maintains a monotonically incrementing epoch counter (wraps); on sampled requests (say 1 in 8), we update the key metadata epoch. Eviction score = base_score * epoch_weight(epoch_age) + sample_hit_bonus. Epoch weights decay exponentially across epochs. This is robust to rapid shifts because recent epochs dominate and old epochs fade out without explicit decrements.
- Low overhead: epoch tag fits alongside existing metadata (compact bitfield), updates are relaxed and can be applied with CAS on metadata word.

Memory pool layout and integration

- Shard topology: keep existing shard-per-GPU model. Each shard owns a few ADS delta-sets (hot, warm, cold) rather than a single monolithic slab table. Delta-sets share a compact free-list ring and a small churn log for background compaction.
- Metadata word layout (64-bit): [48-bit ptr/slotid] [12-bit epoch/tag] [4-bit flags]. We experimented with 12-bit epoch and found wrap frequency manageable at 500–2000 qps per worker; reduce to 8-bit for very constrained metadata budgets.
- Overflow region: infrequent large allocations go to an overflow chunk arena backed by host memory pinned and paged; eviction policy tries to prefer spilling overflow entries first.

Eviction heuristics and mixing ADS+EH

- Admission: new item admitted into a candidate delta-class if space; otherwise use immediate preferential eviction candidates (oldest epoch, low sample-hit count) across classes. This avoids high-cost compaction on admission.
- Eviction selection: compute a lightweight score S = (size_penalty * size_in_bytes) - (epoch_boost * recentness) - (sample_hits * hit_boost). Target to minimize bytes-freed-per-eviction while preserving hot prefixes.
- Compaction: background compactor operates on shard churn logs, attempting to coalesce partially-used adjacent slots when doing relocation to warm delta-sets. Compaction is rate-limited and backoff-aware.

Experiment design and metrics

Workloads simulated:
- Mix A: 60% short chat contexts (~1–2KB), 30% medium (8–32KB), 10% embeddings (64–256KB) with bursty tenant traffic (tenants switch hotness every 30–90s).
- Mix B: long-tail access with many cold keys and occasional re-heating events (simulating cache-warmups after model rollouts).

Key metrics to capture:
- Internal fragmentation: average wasted bytes per entry, and fraction of free-space unusable due to size class mismatch.
- Allocation latency P50/P95/P99 (ns/µs) on hot-path.
- Eviction churn: relocations/sec and bytes relocated/sec.
- Hit-rate retention across epoch windows and 99th percentile tail latencies for requests that require allocations.
- Cost metric: bytes freed per eviction and net effective capacity increase.

Results (summary of small cluster sim)

- ADS + EH vs baseline slab allocator: internal fragmentation down by ~28% on Mix A, and ~34% on Mix B. Effective usable capacity increased by ~14–18% for same physical memory.
- Allocation latency: P50 unchanged, P95 increased by ~6% (due to occasional overflow splits), P99 increased by ~12% but remained within our 99.9% SLO targets in controlled tests.
- Eviction relocations: background compaction reduced stop-the-world relocations by ~70%, but increased background throughput by ~8% (acceptable tradeoff).
- Epochal hinting significantly reduced eviction of recently re-accessed prefixes during tenant switches — measured as 3x lower prefix miss amplification in the first 10s after a tenant becomes hot.

Operational concerns and open questions

- Epoch wrap and aliasing: small epoch tags can alias, producing false recentness. We need to tune epoch increment frequency to worker QPS and consider per-tenant epoch namespaces for noisy tenants.
- Worst-case adversarial patterns: very large allocations interleaved with tiny allocations could still induce fragmentation; overflow arena policies need refinement (e.g., prefer evicting a set of small items instead of allocating a single huge overflow chunk when feasible).
- Cross-shard rebalancing: we didn’t explore cross-shard migration for long-lived hot keys. That may further improve utilization but introduces complexity.
- Implementation complexity: ADS requires careful lock-free free-list design to avoid allocation stalls at high concurrency. Early prototype used thin per-thread caches to avoid contention; more rigorous stress testing required.

Next steps and implementation plan

1) Prototype in a feature-flagged branch: implement ADS delta computation (sampling, t-digest), epoch tagging, and scoring. Use existing shard APIs and per-worker epoch counters. (Owner: Samir)
2) Microbench harness: extend current allocator simulator to run Mix A/B at 10–100x production QPS to observe P99 behavior. (Owner: Nina)
3) Safety net: expose runtime knobs — delta-class count, epoch sampling rate, compactor bandwidth — to allow on-the-fly tuning. (Owner: Lena)
4) Dashboarding: add metrics for internal fragmentation, epoch distribution heatmap, relocation bytes/sec, overflow allocations. Visualize per-tenant breakdown.

Decision criteria for rollout

- If prototype yields >10% usable capacity improvement with <20% P99 allocation latency regression and no increase in observed request tail latency at the API level, proceed to canary on Dedicated GPU nodes behind feature flag.

Appendix: quick pseudo-spec snippets

- Delta recompute (pseudo):
  sample_sizes = reservoir_sample(1024)
  quantiles = tdigest(sample_sizes)
  choose k class boundaries that minimize sum(expected_waste) subject to k<=K_MAX (greedy k-means on 1D sizes)
  publish new delta-set atomically

- Epoch scoring (pseudo):
  on_sampled_access(key): metadata.epoch = worker_epoch
  eviction_score(key) = size * size_penalty - (now_epoch - metadata.epoch) * epoch_boost - metadata.sample_hits * hit_boost

Notes and links

- Related discussion threads: slack #serving-runtime: "delta-slotting idea" (2024-10), design review notes (CONF-321).
- Prototype PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/4123

Notes-to-self

- Keep initial delta-class count small (4–6). Avoid aggressive automatic class churn in first rollout.
- Make compactor cooperative with normal request path to avoid tail spikes.
