- Low overhead: epoch tag fits alongside existing metadata (compact bitfield), updates are relaxed and can be applied with CAS on metadata word.

Memory pool layout and integration

- Shard topology: keep existing shard-per-GPU model. Each shard owns a few ADS delta-sets (hot, warm, cold) rather than a single monolithic slab table. Delta-sets share a compact free-list ring and a small churn log for background compaction.
- Metadata word layout (64-bit): [48-bit ptr/slotid] [12-bit epoch/tag] [4-bit flags]. We experimented with 12-bit epoch and found wrap frequency manageable at 500–2000 qps per worker; reduce to 8-bit for very constrained metadata budgets.
- Overflow region: infrequent large allocations go to an overflow chunk arena backed by host memory pinned and paged; eviction policy tries to prefer spilling overflow entries first.

Eviction heuristics and mixing ADS+EH

- Admission: new item admitted into a candidate delta-class if space; otherwise use immediate preferential eviction candidates (oldest epoch, low sample-hit count) across classes. This avoids high-cost compaction on admission.
- Eviction selection: compute a lightweight score S = (size_penalty * size_in_bytes) - (epoch_boost * recentness) - (sample_hits * hit_boost). Target to minimize bytes-freed-per-eviction while preserving hot prefixes.
- Compaction: background compactor operates on shard churn logs, attempting to coalesce partially-used adjacent slots when doing relocation to warm delta-sets. Compaction is rate-limited and backoff-aware.

Experiment design and metrics

Workloads simulated:
- Mix A: 60% short chat contexts (~1–2KB), 30% medium (8–32KB), 10% embeddings (64–256KB) with bursty tenant traffic (tenants switch hotness every 30–90s).
- Mix B: long-tail access with many cold keys and occasional re-heating events (simulating cache-warmups after model rollouts).

Key metrics to capture:
- Internal fragmentation: average wasted bytes per entry, and fraction of free-space unusable due to size class mismatch.
- Allocation latency P50/P95/P99 (ns/µs) on hot-path.
- Eviction churn: relocations/sec and bytes relocated/sec.
- Hit-rate retention across epoch windows and 99th percentile tail latencies for requests that require allocations.
- Cost metric: bytes freed per eviction and net effective capacity increase.

Results (summary of small cluster sim)

- ADS + EH vs baseline slab allocator: internal fragmentation down by ~28% on Mix A, and ~34% on Mix B. Effective usable capacity increased by ~14–18% for same physical memory.
- Allocation latency: P50 unchanged, P95 increased by ~6% (due to occasional overflow splits), P99 increased by ~12% but remained within our 99.9% SLO targets in controlled tests.
- Eviction relocations: background compaction reduced stop-the-world relocations by ~70%, but increased background throughput by ~8% (acceptable tradeoff).
- Epochal hinting significantly reduced eviction of recently re-accessed prefixes during tenant switches — measured as 3x lower prefix miss amplification in the first 10s after a tenant becomes hot.

Operational concerns and open questions

- Epoch wrap and aliasing: small epoch tags can alias, producing false recentness. We need to tune epoch increment frequency to worker QPS and consider per-tenant epoch namespaces for noisy tenants.
- Worst-case adversarial patterns: very large allocations interleaved with tiny allocations could still induce fragmentation; overflow arena policies need refinement (e.g., prefer evicting a set of small items instead of allocating a single huge overflow chunk when feasible).
- Cross-shard rebalancing: we didn’t explore cross-shard migration for long-lived hot keys. That may further improve utilization but introduces complexity.
- Implementation complexity: ADS requires careful lock-free free-list design to avoid allocation stalls at high concurrency. Early prototype used thin per-thread caches to avoid contention; more rigorous stress testing required.

Next steps and implementation plan

1) Prototype in a feature-flagged branch: implement ADS delta computation (sampling, t-digest), epoch tagging, and scoring. Use existing shard APIs and per-worker epoch counters. (Owner: Samir)
2) Microbench harness: extend current allocator simulator to run Mix A/B at 10–100x production QPS to observe P99 behavior. (Owner: Nina)
3) Safety net: expose runtime knobs — delta-class count, epoch sampling rate, compactor bandwidth — to allow on-the-fly tuning. (Owner: Lena)
4) Dashboarding: add metrics for internal fragmentation, epoch distribution heatmap, relocation bytes/sec, overflow allocations. Visualize per-tenant breakdown.

Decision criteria for rollout

- If prototype yields >10% usable capacity improvement with <20% P99 allocation latency regression and no increase in observed request tail latency at the API level, proceed to canary on Dedicated GPU nodes behind feature flag.

Appendix: quick pseudo-spec snippets

- Delta recompute (pseudo):
  sample_sizes = reservoir_sample(1024)
  quantiles = tdigest(sample_sizes)
  choose k class boundaries that minimize sum(expected_waste) subject to k<=K_MAX (greedy k-means on 1D sizes)
  publish new delta-set atomically

- Epoch scoring (pseudo):
  on_sampled_access(key): metadata.epoch = worker_epoch
  eviction_score(key) = size * size_penalty - (now_epoch - metadata.epoch) * epoch_boost - metadata.sample_hits * hit_boost

Notes and links

- Related discussion threads: slack #serving-runtime: "delta-slotting idea" (2024-10), design review notes (CONF-321).
- Prototype PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/serving-runtime/pull/4123

Notes-to-self

- Keep initial delta-class count small (4–6). Avoid aggressive automatic class churn in first rollout.
- Make compactor cooperative with normal request path to avoid tail spikes.
text:
Summary

This doc captures a set of small-scale experiments exploring two complementary techniques to reduce KV cache fragmentation and lower tail latency under mixed workloads: (1) adaptive delta slotting — a dynamic variable-slot allocator that adjusts slot grain on per-key-size distributions, and (2) epochal hinting — a lightweight metadata epoch per request that biases retention/eviction without heavy global counters. The goal: materially reduce memory waste (measured as unusable internal fragmentation) while keeping eviction cost and allocation latency low enough for 99.9% SLOs.

Background and motivation

We’ve been seeing two recurring issues: (a) high internal fragmentation from uniform slot sizes when serving a mix of short and long KV blobs (e.g., chat contexts vs. embeddings), and (b) eviction thrash when tenant request patterns change rapidly (bursty prefix access). Existing slab-like pools reduce external fragmentation but still leave many partially-used slots across tenants. Full compaction and stop-the-world GC are expensive. We need incremental, low-latency techniques that adapt to workload skew and temporal locality.

High-level approach

1) Adaptive delta slotting (ADS)
- Idea: maintain multiple slot \"deltas\" per shard where a delta is a small set of slot size classes but the mapping of keys -> class is probabilistic and adjustable. Instead of fixed slab bins (16B, 32B, 64B, ...), ADS tracks an empirical size histogram per shard and then selects a compact set of bucket widths that minimize worst-case internal waste under a constrained number of classes (e.g., 6 classes).
- Runtime behavior: on allocation, probe active delta classes; if none fit the request cheaply, ADS will (a) fast-path split a larger free slot (if available), or (b) fallback to an overflow compacting allocation (rare). A background thread recomputes the delta set every epoch window (configurable, 1–10s) using a lightweight quantile approximation (t-digest variant) to avoid locking hot paths.
- Goals: reduce average unused bytes-per-entry and reduce cross-tenant interferences by making slot classes match observed workload. Lower-cost in-place split/merge operations instead of heavy relocation.

2) Epochal hinting (EH)
- Idea: attach a tiny epoch id (8-12 bits) to requests served by a worker; when a key is referenced, its metadata stores the current epoch tag. Eviction/retention heuristics use epoch-tag recency and cross-epoch hit counts to estimate short-term hotness without maintaining heavy LRU lists or global timestamps.
- Mechanics: serve loop maintains a monotonically incrementing epoch counter (wraps); on sampled requests (say 1 in 8), we update the key metadata epoch. Eviction score = base_score * epoch_weight(epoch_age) + sample_hit_bonus. Epoch weights decay exponentially across epochs. This is robust to rapid shifts because recent epochs dominate and old epochs fade out without explicit decrements.
- Low overhead: epoch tag fits alongside existing metadata (compact bitfield), updates are relaxed and can be applied with CAS on metadata word.

- Metadata word layout (64-bit): [48-bit ptr/slotid] [12-bit epoch/tag] [4-bit flags]. We experimented with 12-bit epoch and found wrap frequency manageable at 500–2000 qps per worker; reduce to 8-bit for very constrained metadata budgets.
- Overflow region: infrequent large allocations go to an overflow chunk arena backed by host memory pinned and paged; eviction policy tries to prefer spilling overflow entries first.

- Eviction selection: compute a lightweight score S = (size_penalty * size_in_bytes) - (epoch_boost * recentness) - (sample_hits * hit_boost). Target to minimize bytes-freed-per-eviction while preserving hot prefixes.
- Compaction: background compactor operates on shard churn logs, attempting to coalesce partially-used adjacent slots when doing relocation to warm delta-sets. Compaction is rate-limited and backoff-aware.

Experiment design and metrics

Workloads simulated:
- Mix A: 60% short chat contexts (~1–2KB), 30% medium (8–32KB), 10% embeddings (64–256KB) with bursty tenant traffic (tenants switch hotness every 30–90s).
- Mix B: long-tail access with many cold keys and occasional re-heating events (simulating cache-warmups after model rollouts).

Key metrics to capture:
- Internal fragmentation: average wasted bytes per entry, and fraction of free-space unusable due to size class mismatch.
- Allocation latency P50/P95/P99 (ns/µs) on hot-path.
- Eviction churn: relocations/sec and bytes relocated/sec.
- Hit-rate retention across epoch windows and 99th percentile tail latencies for requests that require allocations.
- Cost metric: bytes freed per eviction and net effective capacity increase.

Results (summary of small cluster sim)

- ADS + EH vs baseline slab allocator: internal fragmentation down by ~28% on Mix A, and ~34% on Mix B. Effective usable capacity increased by ~14–18% for same physical memory.

- Eviction relocations: background compaction reduced stop-the-world relocations by ~70%, but increased background throughput by ~8% (acceptable tradeoff).
- Epochal hinting significantly reduced eviction of recently re-accessed prefixes during tenant switches — measured as 3x lower prefix miss amplification in the first 10s after a tenant becomes hot.

Operational concerns and open questions

- Epoch wrap and aliasing: small epoch tags can alias, producing false recentness. We need to tune epoch increment frequency to worker QPS and consider per-tenant epoch namespaces for noisy tenants.
- Worst-case adversarial patterns: very large allocations interleaved with tiny allocations could still induce fragmentation; overflow arena policies need refinement (e.g., prefer evicting a set of small items instead of allocating a single huge overflow chunk when feasible).
- Cross-shard rebalancing: we didn’t explore cross-shard migration for long-lived hot keys. That may further improve utilization but introduces complexity.
- Implementation complexity: ADS requires careful lock-free free-list design to avoid allocation stalls at high concurrency. Early prototype used thin per-thread caches to avoid contention; more rigorous stress testing required.

  quantiles = tdigest(sample_sizes)
  choose k class boundaries that minimize sum(expected_waste) subject to k<=K_MAX (greedy k-means on 1D sizes)

- Make compactor cooperative with normal request path to avoid tail spikes.

source.title: microstructure-selllab-playlets-draft
source.content:
Purpose:
Quick, reusable "playlets" (short script + micro-deck snippets) for sellers and SEs to run pilots, manage canaries, and execute graceful fallbacks during early production migrations. This doc is an authoring sketch — lean on it to generate a 6–8 slide deck + one-pager cheat card for field use.

Audience:
- Solutions Engineers (primary)
- Account Execs / Platform Sellers (secondary)
- Customer SRE/Platform contacts during pilot handoff

Core thesis (single line):
Make the pilot safe, measurably low-risk, and fast to iterate by packaging: a) narrow success criteria, b) simple rollback/fallback tactics, c) transparent economics, and d) an SE checklist that fits inside a 10-minute conversation.

Recommended slide flow (6 slides + appendix):
1) 30s hook: pilot outcome + what success looks like (latency SLO, cost target, sample user story)
   - Speaker note: open with customer persona + one metric that matters (e.g., sub-200ms 95p for chat snippet).
2) Pilot scope: traffic, datatypes, model choices, timeframe
   - Callouts: safe-sample size (<=5% of sessions), fallback route (cached responses/low-cost model), rollback window
3) Operational playlets (the new bit)
   - 3 distinct playlets: rapid-canary, degraded-mode-fallback, graceful-model-swap
   - Each playlet = 2 lines: trigger condition, immediate seller/SE script, 3-step execution checklist
4) Economics snapshot (unit math + worst-case cap)
   - Quick table: tokens/req, tokens/day@pilot, monthly burn ceiling, recommended quota guardrails
5) Handoff checklist to customer SRE / platform team
   - Items: routing policy, metric hooks (latency, error-rate), log retention, rollback URL, contact points
6) Next steps & gating criteria to expand beyond pilot
   - Criteria: sustained SLOs for 72 hours, <X% quality regressions on eval set, cost within Y% of forecast
Appendix: quick troubleshooting scripts, sample Slack messages, minimal tech diagram, ticket templates

Playlet details (example content for slide #3):
- Rapid-canary playlet:
  Trigger: new model variant or config pushed to pilot path and 1%+ latency spike OR 5% p50 regression on eval set
  Seller script (2-line): "We’re opening 1–2% traffic to the new config for 48h. If latency or regressions appear we flip to the cached fallback while we triage — you’ll be in the loop and we’ll revert within 30 minutes if conditions persist."
  SE checklist: enable canary flag, set quota to 2% sessions, enable latency alarm (95p), confirm fallback route, schedule 15m post-open check-in

- Degraded-mode-fallback playlet:
  Trigger: sustained throughput saturation or autoscaler lag causing tail latency breaches
  Seller script: "We’ll route session subsets to a low-cost small model and enable KV prefix caching; this keeps UX alive while we scale. Expect brief differences in wording but not in intent."
  SE checklist: set model routing policy, flip small-model endpoint, apply cache TTL changes, update dashboard annotation

- Graceful-model-swap playlet:
  Trigger: confirmed quality regression after a rollout OR infra pressure on primary model
  Seller script: "We’ll switch to the prior verified model variant and open an internal postmortem; we’ll preserve logs and prompt traces for the triage team."
  SE checklist: validate previous model artifact id, apply routing override, mark incident ticket with request ids

Speaker scripts & short rebuttals (one-liners):
- Objection: "We’re worried about hallucinations."
  Response: "We’ll ship a narrow prompt guard and run parallel sampling to compare hallucination rates for the pilot cohort — if we see drift we’ll pause the pilot and surface examples within 24h."
- Objection: "This will cost us money."
  Response: "Pilot math is explicit: max burn ceiling and auto-quota. We set hard caps and a kill switch — you control cost exposure."

Operational knobs to pre-configure before pilot kickoff:
- Latency SLI thresholds (p50, p95, p99), error rate thresholds, model fallback routes, quota gates, and alert runbooks.
- Tagging conventions: pilot=<account>-<pilot-id>, route=canary|primary|fallback (used in logs and tracing)

Metrics to collect (minimum):
- User-facing: p50/p95 latency, successful response rate, prompt tokens/resp tokens, embed/stream error rate
- Quality: eval-set pass rate (automated), human-sampled quality check (10/day)
- Cost: token consumption by model variant, total pilot spend vs ceiling

One-pager cheat card (to be produced):
- Top half: What to say (30s pitch + 2 seller rebuttals)
- Bottom half: Execution cheats (3 playlets with 3-step checklists and autocalled Jira templates)

Outstanding questions / decisions we need to make in next draft:
- Naming convention: playlets vs playbooks? (preference: playlets = 1–2 min actionable items)
- Which exact quota enforcement UX do we ship in Console before enabling this at scale? (ask product/console team)
- Do we want a templated GitHub PR that wires up default alerts and tags? (ops-engineering input)

Next steps / sprint plan (authors):
- This week: convert to slide deck + one-pager cheat card (owner: Ethan)
- Next week: review with SEs (Priya, Liam) and Product (Sofia) — capture feedback from 3 mock calls
- Week after: iterate, produce final seller pocket PDF and GitHub repo with ticket templates

Quick refs & artifacts to copy into final deck:
- BF: pilot ticket template (JIRA-GTM-7123)
- Playbook skeleton in Confluence: confluence:INF-2034-microstructure-playlets
- Example PR wiring alerts: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sales-assets/pull/88

Notes to self (rough):
- Keep language simple, avoid deep runtime jargon for AE-facing slides (use in appendix)
- Make the 30s hook metric-first (one number wins attention)
- We should ship a short Loom demo walkthrough for SE onboarding

Writer: Ethan Park (please iterate), review: Priya Nair, Liam O'Connell, Sofia Martinez

End of sketch.

- Do we want a templated GitHub PR that wires up default alerts and tags? (ops-engineering input)

Next steps / sprint plan (authors):
- This week: convert to slide deck + one-pager cheat card (owner: Ethan)
- Next week: review with SEs (Priya, Liam) and Product (Sofia) — capture feedback from 3 mock calls
- Week after: iterate, produce final seller pocket PDF and GitHub repo with ticket templates

Quick refs & artifacts to copy into final deck:
- BF: pilot ticket template (JIRA-GTM-7123)
- Playbook skeleton in Confluence: confluence:INF-2034-microstructure-playlets
- Example PR wiring alerts: https://github.com/redwood-inference/sales-assets/pull/88

Notes to self (rough):
- Keep language simple, avoid deep runtime jargon for AE-facing slides (use in appendix)
- Make the 30s hook metric-first (one number wins attention)
- We should ship a short Loom demo walkthrough for SE onboarding

Writer: Ethan Park (please iterate), review: Priya Nair, Liam O'Connell, Sofia Martinez

End of sketch.

source.title: Sealed environment artifact workflow — TF rough draft
source.summary: Working draft: end-to-end playbook for creating, delivering, installing, and upgrading Redwood Private in sealed (air-gapped) customer environments. Contains a proposed bundle manifest, verification checklist, Terraform module surface notes for mirror/proxy, and a staged upgrade strategy with rollback considerations.
source.content:
Status: draft — notes, TODOs, and experimental commands ahead.

Context and goals
- Target: customers that cannot reach public registries (air-gapped / sealed VPCs).
- Deliverable: a repeatable, testable offline bundle + operator pattern that:
  - populates an internal OCI registry or registry proxy,
  - provides a verified artifact index and checksums,
  - exposes a compact terraform module interface so Solutions Eng can seed registries during install,
  - supports safe rolling upgrades with minimal downtime.

Design constraints and assumptions
- No direct internet egress from customer cluster during install/upgrade.
- Operators/agents run in an admin network segment with controlled upload channel (sneaker-net, S3 to transfer station, or customer-provided bastion).
- Primary artifacts: container images, Helm charts, K8s CRDs, operator manifests, model artifact tarballs (optional), and license/policy files.
- Cryptographic signing required (cosign) and KMS/HSM integration recommended for production installs.

Bundle format (proposal)
- Top-level tar.gz: redwood-offline-<version>-<arch>.tar.gz
- Inside:
  - manifest.yaml  # JSON/YAML index of artifacts, sizes, checksums, signatures
  - images/       # directory with OCI tarballs (.tar) saved via 'skopeo' or 'docker save'
  - charts/       # helm tars or simple chart directories
  - yaml/         # supplemental k8s manifests (CRDs, RBAC)
  - models/       # optional model archives, with metadata.json per model
  - tools/        # helper binaries (mirror-agent, checksum-verifier)

Manifest example (manifest.yaml excerpt)
- version: 1
- package: redwood-private
- release: 2024.11.0
- artifacts:
  - name: redwood/controller
    type: image
    file: images/redwood-controller.tar
    sha256: <sha256sum>
    size: 214748364
  - name: redwood/web-console
    type: image
    file: images/web-console.tar
    sha256: <sha256sum>
  - name: redwood/platform-crds
    type: manifest
    file: yaml/platform-crds.yaml
    sha256: <sha256sum>

Signing and verification
- Use cosign to sign each OCI image and the bundle manifest. Provide both a detached signature and a root cert chain in tools/signing/.
- Verification flow on target: verify manifest signature, then verify sha256 of each file after extraction before attempting load.
- If KMS/HSM is in use, store verification public keys in the control-plane vault and validate chain before install.

Delivery channels (customer options)
- S3-transfer station -> customer VPN pull (preferred for scale).
- USB / air-gap courier for extremely isolated sites (use checksummed, signed media).
- Customer-hosted mirror: create a reproducible mirror tarball that the customer's registry team can import.

Mirror/Registry seeding pattern notes (Terraform surface)
- We propose a minimal Terraform module: redwood/mirror-seed
  - Inputs:
    - registry_endpoint (string)  # address for customer private registry
    - credentials_secret_id (string)  # reference to secret manager or KMS alias
    - bundle_source (string)  # S3 path or local path to tar.gz
    - import_strategy (enum: push|pull|skopeo)
    - storage_class (string)  # optional for intermediate storage in cluster
  - Outputs:
    - seed_job_name (string)
    - seeded_digest_map_url (string)  # location of generated digest map after seeding

- Implementation notes: the module should create a Kubernetes Job (or controller) that runs the mirror-agent to: extract the bundle, validate checksums, and push images to the customer registry. If import_strategy == pull we create short-lived credentials for the customer's registry to pull from the transfer station.

Terraform module TODOs (rough list)
- Add explicit retry/backoff knobs for each push operation (mirror-agent will exponential backoff).
- Expose preflight checks as a plan-only mode (validate manifest vs registry available names).
- Provide an "audit mode" which records each pushed artifact (digest, timestamp, filesize) into a GCS/S3-compatible storage for post-install verification.
- Support both Docker v2 registry and Harbor/Quay enterprise features (namespace creation, project-level quotas).

Upgrade plan (staged, low-risk)
- Goals: avoid full cluster disruption, enable quick rollback to prior patch level, preserve operator state and model artifact references.
- Stages:
  1) Preflight: run manifest verification, validate cluster resource requests, check PV capacity, ensure cluster autoscaler ready.
  2) Canary seed: push images for controller and a single non-critical chart (e.g., metrics exporter) to registry and run in canary namespace. Smoke test.
  3) Operator rolling update: update controller images with maxUnavailable=1, monitor reconciliation loops and error metrics.
  4) Core data-plane rollout: update inference-serving pods with progressive batch sizes and observe latency SLOs.
  5) Post-upgrade verification: run prompt-sets and evals (subset of Redwood Optimize checks) to detect regressions.
  6) Finalize: mark release in control-plane and notify customer.

Rollback considerations
- Keep previous image bundle available locally in cluster storage for immediate re-push.
- If CRD conversion is required, provide conversion hooks in the bundle (migration jobs) that can be run with --dry-run first.
- Snapshot operator state (etcd snapshot or operator-managed state export) before steps 3+4.

Testing checklist (pre-install and pre-upgrade)
- [ ] verify manifest signature chain
- [ ] verify sha256s for each archive in the bundle
- [ ] ensure target registry accepts pushed content and returns digests
- [ ] PV capacity check for unpacking artifacts (estimate: ~5GB + sum(images))
- [ ] run smoke helm install in a canary namespace that points to local registry
- [ ] run simulated network partition tests (mirror-agent resume behavior)

Operational runbook (operator target side)
1) Transfer bundle to transfer-station host (SCP/S3 import).
2) Run: tar xzf redwood-offline-<version>.tar.gz -C /var/tmp/redwood-install
3) tools/checksum-verify /var/tmp/redwood-install/manifest.yaml
4) If verification OK, apply terraform module with bundle_source set to /var/tmp/redwood-install or S3 path.
5) Monitor seed job logs: kubectl logs -f job/seed-redwood-xxx
6) After seed completes, run /tools/validate-seed --digest-map <url> to confirm each artifact is present.

Common failure modes and mitigations
- Push timeouts: increase mirror-agent timeout and enable chunked uploads.
- Partial imports: module should mark failed artifacts and allow a resume-from-manifest-index.
- Corrupt media (USB): always check sha256 and keep transfer checksum log; request re-shipment if mismatch.

Security and compliance notes
- All bundles must be signed with customer-accepted key. Provide cosign + public key in the initial handoff docs.
- Secrets are never included in bundle. Terraform module should accept secret references to external secret stores only.
- Consider offline attestation for critical components (controller) using TPM/HSM in very high-assurance customers.

Open questions / TODOs
- Do we keep model artifacts in the same bundle or a separate model-only bundle? Pros/cons to be evaluated for size and frequency of updates.
- How to best support incremental deltas (send only changed images) vs full bundle for each patch release?
- UX: a single CLI that wraps bundle creation, cosign signing, and upload to transfer station. Prototype exists at linked PR but needs polishing.

Quick commands (examples)
- Create image tar: skopeo copy --format=v2s2 docker://registry/repo:tag oci-archive:images/repo-tag.tar
- Sign manifest: cosign sign --key cosign.key manifest.yaml
- Verify manifest: cosign verify --key cosign.pub manifest.yaml.sig
- Load image into local registry: crane push images/repo-tag.tar docker://private.registry/repo:tag

Notes to reviewers:
- This document is intentionally verbose and pragmatic. Please comment on: bundle layout, Terraform input surface, and the staged upgrade approach.
- Priya: can you add storage estimate formulas for image extraction?
- Liam: can you review the TF module retry semantics and suggest knobs?
- Zoe: please sanity-check signing and KMS/HSM recommendations against security guidelines.

End of draft.

Revision history:
- 2024-07-15: initial draft (Ethan)
- 2024-09-03: added manifest example and signing notes (Priya)
- 2024-11-21: expanded upgrade plan and rollback considerations (Liam / Zoe)

source.title: Microburst latency fingerprint notes
source.content:
Context and why this doc exists:

We observed several short-duration latency spikes (microbursts) in the hosted fleet during early Nov 2024 that show up as narrow but high-magnitude p99 excursions in traces for a subset of workloads. This note collects an initial fingerprint of those microbursts, a hypothesis matrix, sampling instructions, and suggested mitigations to test. Unlike the broader long-tail studies, these spikes are transient (100ms-3s windows) and correlate with bursty arrival patterns and certain batching states.

Summary of observed behavior (high level):
- Frequency: ~2-5 microbursts per hour per pod for affected pods (traffic pattern: bursty chat workloads, multi-turn).
- Duration: median 450ms, 90th 930ms, max ~2.6s (measured in e2e traces).
- Affected quantiles: minimal movement at p50 and p95; p99 and p999 show pronounced spikes.
- Rough impact: for a cohort of 320k requests/day, microbursts increase 99th token latency cost by ~12% and cause tail-driven autoscaler oscillations in a few clusters.

Representative aggregate quantiles (baseline windows vs microburst windows):
- Baseline (no microburst): p50=85ms, p95=220ms, p99=410ms.
- Microburst snapshot: p50=92ms, p95=240ms, p99=1,420ms.
- Token-level emission during microburst windows shows long pauses between token emissions (token delta distribution shifts right).

Per-stage breakdown (fields captured from our trace schema):
Stages: ingress -> auth -> routing -> batch-coordinator -> kv-cache-lookup -> model-exec (kernel stages) -> kv-append -> token-emit-serialize -> egress
Typical stage attribution during a microburst (sample p99 trace):
- ingress: 8ms
- auth: 3ms
- routing: 15ms
- batch-coordinator (queue wait + merging): 520ms
- kv-cache-lookup (miss + remote fetch): 240ms
- model-exec (stalling on kernel scheduling): 320ms
- kv-append (update cache): 40ms
- token-emit-serialize: 12ms
- egress: 5ms
Notes: batch-coordinator and kv-cache-lookup dominate here (combined ~760ms). Model-exec also shows non-trivial extra time compared to baseline, likely from GPU scheduling jitter when a large batch suddenly arrives.

Fingerprint patterns we are seeing (bucketed):
1) Queue-amplified microburst (most common): many small incoming requests hit an idle batcher, get coalesced into an unexpectedly large merge window causing a long queue wait. Signature: large batch size at time of emission, batch-coordinator wait >> model-exec.
2) KV-miss cascade: high prefix diversity triggers multiple remote KV lookups in the same micro-window; remote datastore latency amplifies tail. Signature: kv-cache-lookup >> model-exec, external datastore traces correlated.
3) GPU-scheduling jitter: GPU context-switch/eviction events cause per-request kernel launch delay; signature: model-exec > baseline despite small batch sizes, kernel start latency high.
4) Mixed pathology: combination of (1) + (2) where batch size + remote lookups multiply.

Sampling & repro instructions (how to find more traces):
1) Use trace sampler with window filter: tag traces where e2e.latency > 800ms && e2e.latency < 4000ms and request.rate_change > 3x within 1s.
2) Narrow by pod label: serving-group=chat-burst-2024-nov and region=us-west1.
3) Pull top 200 traces by e2e.latency and export stage-level timings.
4) Run the lightweight tracer tool (scripts/trace-export.sh) with flags: --min-latency-ms 800 --lookback 2h --include-stage-ticks. Example: ./scripts/trace-export.sh --svc chat-model --min-latency-ms 800 --lookback 2h --out /tmp/microburst-sample.ndjson

Quick command snippets used in analysis:
- jq filter for stage slice: jq '.stages | map({name, duration_ms})' microburst-sample.ndjson | less
- sample-by-batch-size: jq -r '.meta.batch_size' microburst-sample.ndjson | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr
- correlate kv rpc: rg "kv-lookup" /var/log/traces | sed -n '1,200p'

Hypothesis matrix (what could cause each pattern):
- Queue-amplified microburst: batcher merge timer/ms backlog heuristics mis-tuned for bursty request inter-arrival; autoscaler or pod sleep causes transient backlog.
- KV-miss cascade: cache TTLs + prefix explosion + cold-partition skew; external datastore SLO occasionally slips under burst.
- GPU-scheduling jitter: co-located batch eviction, driver-level preemption, or host scheduler preemption for maintenance tasks.

Immediate mitigation experiments to run (short-running, low-risk):
A) Reduce max merge window from 25ms -> 12ms for chat-heavy routes behind an experiment flag. Measure p99 delta over 6h.
B) Force KV cache warmup for a test cohort (pre-warm prefixes on first request), track kv-lookup miss rate and tail.
C) Isolate a small set of pods to dedicated GPU queue (no host co-tenants) for 24h to test GPU-scheduling hypothesis.
D) Add temporary token-level backpressure (cap concurrent in-flight tokens per connection) to see if that reduces batch-amplification.

Longer-term work items (if experiments validate):
- Adaptive merge windows: heuristic based on current inter-arrival distribution rather than static timer.
- Staggered KV fetches + speculative prefetch for chats with predictably long prefixes.
- Scheduler-level tuning (driver + cgroups) to reduce kernel-launch jitter; investigate using persistent model contexts pinned to GPU.

Metrics to capture for A/B tests:
- p50/p95/p99 e2e latency (token-level and request-level) for control vs experiment
- batch size distribution (median, 95th, max) over windows
- kv-cache miss rate and remote-kv latency p50/p95/p99
- GPU kernel start latency distribution (kernel_submission -> kernel_first_op)
- autoscaler scale events and pod restart counts

Trace examples included in the export (short IDs):
- trace-20241112-8f3b (p99 1,610ms) -> pattern: queue-amplified
- trace-20241113-a12c (p99 1,420ms) -> pattern: kv-miss cascade
- trace-20241114-4e7d (p99 2,610ms) -> pattern: mixed pathology (batch=48 + multiple kv misses)

Open tickets and owners:
- ENG-1987: "Investigate microburst fingerprint and propose mitigations" (owner: Ethan Park)
- ENG-1993: "Experiment: reduce merge window for chat route" (owner: Priya Nandakumar)
- INFRA-557: "GPU scheduling telemetry improvement (kernel start timings)" (owner: Tomás Silva)

Notes for reviewers:
- This is a working note; expect fragmented traces and raw jq snippets. The goal is to capture reproducible sampling filters and a prioritized experiment list.
- If you add analysis results, please append with date + initials and link the sample trace IDs you examined.

Next immediate steps (this week):
1) Priya -> roll out experiment A (12ms merge window) to 5% traffic today, monitor p99 and batch size distribution for 6h.
2) Tomás -> enable extra kernel start timing instrumentation on a staging GPU pool; share results in ENG-1987.
3) Lina -> coordinate with storage team to surface remote KV latency histograms for the suspect time ranges (Nov 10–14).

Appendix: quick notes on interpreting the traces
- Watch for correlated spikes in batch-coordinator.wait and kv-lookup.duration — they indicate amplification rather than pure model stalls.
- Kernel-level jitter often shows up as multiple small waits inside model-exec rather than one big contiguous block. Look for "kernel_submission" -> "first_op" gaps.
- When batch size is >> median, split traces by origin (same-caller multiplexing vs independent callers) — the mitigation differs.

If you find a microburst trace that doesn't fit the fingerprint above, add it to the "oddball" list in the ticket ENG-1987 with a short note and the trace id.

title: Microburst latency fingerprint notes
preview:
Context and why this doc exists:

We observed several short-duration latency spikes (microbursts) in the hosted fleet during early Nov 2024 that show up as narrow but high-magnitude p99 excursions in traces for a subset of workloads. This note collects an initial fingerprint of those microbursts, a hypothesis matrix, sampling instructions, and suggested mitigations to test. Unlike the broader long-tail studies, these spikes are transient (100ms-3s windows) and correlate with bursty arrival patterns and certain batching states.

Summary of observed behavior (high level):
- Frequency: ~2-5 microbursts per hour per pod for affected pods (traffic pattern: bursty chat workloads, multi-turn).
- Duration: median 450ms, 90th 930ms, max ~2.6s (measured in e2e traces).
- Affected quantiles: minimal movement at p50 and p95; p99 and p999 show pronounced spikes.
- Rough impact: for a cohort of 320k requests/day, microbursts increase 99th token latency cost by ~12% and cause tail-driven autoscaler oscillations in a few clusters.

Representative aggregate quantiles (baseline windows vs microburst windows):
- Baseline (no microburst): p50=85ms, p95=220ms, p99=410ms.
- Microburst snapshot: p50=92ms, p95=240ms, p99=1,420ms.
- Token-level emission during microburst windows shows long pauses between token emissions (token delta distribution shifts right).

Per-stage breakdown (fields captured from our trace schema):
Stages: ingress -> auth -> routing -> batch-coordinator -> kv-cache-lookup -> model-exec (kernel stages) -> kv-append -> token-emit-serialize -> egress
Typical stage attribution during a microburst (sample p99 trace):
- ingress: 8ms
- auth: 3ms
- routing: 15ms
- batch-coordinator (queue wait + merging): 520ms
- kv-cache-lookup (miss + remote fetch): 240ms
- model-exec (stalling on kernel scheduling): 320ms
- kv-append (update cache): 40ms
- token-emit-serialize: 12ms
- egress: 5ms
Notes: batch-coordinator and kv-cache-lookup dominate here (combined ~760ms). Model-exec also shows non-trivial extra time compared to baseline, likely from GPU scheduling jitter when a large batch suddenly arrives.

Fingerprint patterns we are seeing (bucketed):
1) Queue-amplified microburst (most common): many small incoming requests hit an idle batcher, get coalesced into an unexpectedly large merge window causing a long queue wait. Signature: large batch size at time of emission, batch-coordinator wait >> model-exec.
2) KV-miss cascade: high prefix diversity triggers multiple remote KV lookups in the same micro-window; remote datastore latency amplifies tail. Signature: kv-cache-lookup >> model-exec, external datastore traces correlated.
3) GPU-scheduling jitter: GPU context-switch/eviction events cause per-request kernel launch delay; signature: model-exec > baseline despite small batch sizes, kernel start latency high.
4) Mixed pathology: combination of (1) + (2) where batch size + remote lookups multiply.

Sampling & repro instructions (how to find more traces):
1) Use trace sampler with window filter: tag traces where e2e.latency > 800ms && e2e.latency < 4000ms and request.rate_change > 3x within 1s.
2) Narrow by pod label: serving-group=chat-burst-2024-nov and region=us-west1.
3) Pull top 200 traces by e2e.latency and export stage-level timings.
4) Run the lightweight tracer tool (scripts/trace-export.sh) with flags: --min-latency-ms 800 --lookback 2h --include-stage-ticks. Example: ./scripts/trace-export.sh --svc chat-model --min-latency-ms 800 --lookback 2h --out /tmp/microburst-sample.ndjson

Quick command snippets used in analysis:
- jq filter for stage slice: jq '.stages | map({name, duration_ms})' microburst-sample.ndjson | less
- sample-by-batch-size: jq -r '.meta.batch_size' microburst-sample.ndjson | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr
- correlate kv rpc: rg "kv-lookup" /var/log/traces | sed -n '1,200p'

Hypothesis matrix (what could cause each pattern):
- Queue-amplified microburst: batcher merge timer/ms backlog heuristics mis-tuned for bursty request inter-arrival; autoscaler or pod sleep causes transient backlog.
- KV-miss cascade: cache TTLs + prefix explosion + cold-partition skew; external datastore SLO occasionally slips under burst.
- GPU-scheduling jitter: co-located batch eviction, driver-level preemption, or host scheduler preemption for maintenance tasks.

Immediate mitigation experiments to run (short-running, low-risk):
A) Reduce max merge window from 25ms -> 12ms for chat-heavy routes behind an experiment flag. Measure p99 delta over 6h.
B) Force KV cache warmup for a test cohort (pre-warm prefixes on first request), track kv-lookup miss rate and tail.
C) Isolate a small set of pods to dedicated GPU queue (no host co-tenants) for 24h to test GPU-scheduling hypothesis.
D) Add temporary token-level backpressure (cap concurrent in-flight tokens per connection) to see if that reduces batch-amplification.

Longer-term work items (if experiments validate):
- Adaptive merge windows: heuristic based on current inter-arrival distribution rather than static timer.
- Staggered KV fetches + speculative prefetch for chats with predictably long prefixes.
- Scheduler-level tuning (driver + cgroups) to reduce kernel-launch jitter; investigate using persistent model contexts pinned to GPU.

Metrics to capture for A/B tests:
- p50/p95/p99 e2e latency (token-level and request-level) for control vs experiment
- batch size distribution (median, 95th, max) over windows
- kv-cache miss rate and remote-kv latency p50/p95/p99
- GPU kernel start latency distribution (kernel_submission -> kernel_first_op)
- autoscaler scale events and pod restart counts

Trace examples included in the export (short IDs):
- trace-20241112-8f3b (p99 1,610ms) -> pattern: queue-amplified
- trace-20241113-a12c (p99 1,420ms) -> pattern: kv-miss cascade
- trace-20241114-4e7d (p99 2,610ms) -> pattern: mixed pathology (batch=48 + multiple kv misses)

Open tickets and owners:
- ENG-1987: \"Investigate microburst fingerprint and propose mitigations\" (owner: Ethan Park)
- ENG-1993: \"Experiment: reduce merge window for chat route\" (owner: Priya Nandakumar)
- INFRA-557: \"GPU scheduling telemetry improvement (kernel start timings)\" (owner: Tomás Silva)

Notes for reviewers:
- This is a working note; expect fragmented traces and raw jq snippets. The goal is to capture reproducible sampling filters and a prioritized experiment list.
- If you add analysis results, please append with date + initials and link the sample trace IDs you examined.

Next immediate steps (this week):
1) Priya -> roll out experiment A (12ms merge window) to 5% traffic today, monitor p99 and batch size distribution for 6h.
2) Tomás -> enable extra kernel start timing instrumentation on a staging GPU pool; share results in ENG-1987.
3) Lina -> coordinate with storage team to surface remote KV latency histograms for the suspect time ranges (Nov 10–14).

Appendix: quick notes on interpreting the traces
- Watch for correlated spikes in batch-coordinator.wait and kv-lookup.duration — they indicate amplification rather than pure model stalls.
- Kernel-level jitter often shows up as multiple small waits inside model-exec rather than one big contiguous block. Look for \"kernel_submission\" -> \"first_op\" gaps.
- When batch size is >> median, split traces by origin (same-caller multiplexing vs independent callers) — the mitigation differs.

If you find a microburst trace that doesn't fit the fingerprint above, add it to the \"oddball\" list in the ticket ENG-1987 with a short note and the trace id.

source.title: HavenBank embedded LLM pilot playbook (scratch)
source.content:
Top-level goal: get a 6–8 week pilot with HavenBank (mid-sized regional bank, core: FIS, 2.6M retail accounts). Focus: contextualized customer support assistant + KYC intake templating to reduce handle time and TTR for first contact resolution.

Quick thesis / ROI hook (for outreach):
- Reduce average contact center handle time by 18–25% through prefix caching + short-context templates.
- Cut repeated prompt calls to core systems by batching and local caching — estimated token reduction 30% vs naive API.
- Avoid data egress / PII risk by offering Private deployment (VPC) or Dedicated with KMS integration.
- Pilot success metric: 15% reduction in AHT (avg handle time) or 3000 saved agent-hours/month for the pilot segment.

Account notes (public & scraped intel):
- HQ: Minneapolis; ~2.6M retail; 12 regional branches in Midwest.
- Tech stack: core = FIS; cloud: AWS (us-east primary), MFA + Okta for SSO.
- Security posture: internal SOC2 Type II, heavy emphasis on PII controls and audit logs.
- Recent initiatives: digital transformation playbook FY24 (press release Aug 2024) mentions exploring "AI assistants for customer support".
- Decision influencers: Head of Digital (Sana Iqbal), Head of Ops (Mark Rivers), Dir. of Security (Leah Moore). Procurement lead: Tim Greer.

Why Redwood is a fit (short bullets for pitch):
- Production-first inference: predictable latency SLOs for live chat + agent assist.
- Private deployment option for PII-sensitive flows (VPC + customer KMS).
- Cost controls: batching/prefix caching reduce token spend (pull numbers in discovery).
- Rollout-friendly: can start hosted dev instance -> Dedicated pilot -> Private if they need on-prem.

Initial outreach plan (sequence):
1) Warm intros: check LinkedIn 1st-degree overlaps (Maya + Rafael).
2) Email 1 (short value + ask for 20m): subject variants below.
3) LinkedIn InMail if no response after 4 days.
4) Follow-up with one-pager + pilot terms after interest.

Email drafts (lightweight, pick tone per persona):
- For Head of Digital (Sana):
Subject: Quick pilot idea to cut contact center handle time by ~20%
Hi Sana — quick note from Ethan at Redwood Inference. We help banks run production-grade LLMs (hosted, dedicated, or private) focused on predictable latency and cost.
I read HavenBank’s digital transformation piece and thought there’s a low-risk pilot we could run to: 1) reduce AHT for chat/phone by ~15–25% and 2) keep PII inside your environment via our Private deployment.
Could we grab 20 minutes to compare notes and I’ll show a 7-slide pilot plan?
Best,
Ethan

- For Dir of Security (Leah):
Subject: PII, KMS, and auditability for LLM pilots
Leah — quick intro: we’ve run Private deployments for regulated customers with customer KMS/HSM integration, full audit logs, and egress controls. Happy to share an architecture note and threat model. 10–15m?

LinkedIn InMail short template:
Hi Leah — Ethan from Redwood Inference. We do private LLM deployments with customer KMS and SOC2-compatible audit trails. Would you be open to a 15m call for a short architecture sketch?

Discovery checklist (what to learn on first call):
- Traffic volume (monthly, peak concurrent chats, average tokens per session)
- Current AHT for channels (phone, chat, email). Baseline metrics.
- What data stays in-bank vs. third-party? Any data that cannot leave VPC?
- Who owns procurement/infra approval? Expected procurement timeline.
- Existing monitoring: do they have per-route latency dashboards? any SRE on-call constraints for pilot?
- Success criteria for pilot (ops vs. cost vs. accuracy).
- Technical contacts: infra lead, security, and sample PII flows to redact/transform.

Pilot scope options (fast to implement):
A) Agent assist + canned KYC template generation (4–6 weeks)
- 1 call center team (50 agents) traffic mirror -> evaluate AHT and handoff quality.
- Provide SDK + small on-prem model or dedicated instance behind VPC.
- Success: 15% drop in AHT, >90% agent satisfaction from NPS survey.
B) Customer-facing chat pilot (6–8 weeks)
- Start with non-sensitive FAQs + product lookup.
- Use routing policy -> fallback to smaller local model for low-latency.
- Success: improved containment rate, reduce escalations by 10%.

Quick technical approach (for conversations with platform/security):
- Option 1: Private deployment (control plane in Redwood, data plane in customer VPC) — KMS/HSM integration, no egress of raw transcripts.
- Option 2: Dedicated instance in AWS account with strict IAM roles and VPC endpoints.
- Kernel/config considerations: choose quantized LLM variant for cost; enable KV caching for repeated phrases (e.g., account-number prompts) to reduce token cost.
- Observability: token-level cost and latency dashboards per-route, audit logs streaming to Splunk/ELK, trace IDs for every request.

Objection handling (common):
- "We can’t send PII to a 3rd party": -> Offer Private deployment + bring-your-own-KMS and an architecture review + SOC2 package + contractual data protection.
- "LLMs make mistakes / hallucinate": -> Use retrieval augmented generation for facts (customer data read-only connectors), quality gates, and human-in-loop fallback policies. Show evals: precision on KYC template extraction >92% on a small labeled set.
- "Cost unknown / token bill": -> Walk through expected token profile, show batching & caching recommendations; propose a fixed-price pilot tranche to remove variable spend risk.

Numbers to have pre-call (ballpark):
- Token estimate example: 10k sessions/month x 250 tokens/session = 2.5M tokens/month. With caching/batching reduce to ~1.7M tokens. At hosted list price ~ $X/token (redacted in external copy) => pilot cost estimate $Y (present on call).
- Latency targets: live agent assist aim: p95 < 250ms for LLM completion after caching; if not achievable, aim for <400ms with fallback.

Pilot legal & procurement touchpoints:
- Need to know legal standard SLAs, privacy addenda, and whether they require data processing agreement + DPA + DPAs for sub-processors.
- Procurement timeline: aim for 4 weeks for pilot SOW signoff if there are no major compliance blockers.

Call outline (15–20m intro call):
1) 2m intros + why I reached out
2) 4–6m high-level pilot idea + quick ROI sketch
3) 6–8m discovery questions (use checklist)
4) 2–3m next steps: tech follow-up, security docs, pilot SOW

Interactive notes / quick things to test on POC:
- Synthetic PCI/PII masking module — test if they can accept redacted inputs.
- KV cache behavior on repeated billing questions (should be near-zero extra tokens).
- Failure-mode test: simulate model fallback -> verify routing + tracing works.

Open questions for internal team:
- Who on Solutions Eng can lead a 2-week full-immersion engineering spike? (Maya indicated availability after 11/15).
- Do we have a pre-made pilot SOW template for regional banks (I think there is one in CONFLUENCE-Redwood-embed-playbook-314)?
- Pricing: propose fixed pilot cap (suggest $15k–25k depending on scope) — confirm with pricing ops.

Next actions (this doc):
- [ ] Run quick lookup for 1st-degree LinkedIn overlaps (Maya)
- [ ] Draft email to Sana + Leah + Mark (Ethan) by 11/05
- [ ] Pull baseline AHT industry benchmarks for regional banks (Rafael)
- [ ] Prepare brief security summary (2 slides) for Leah before call (Maya)

Notes-to-self / spin ideas:
- If they push for lower risk path: propose a 2-week discovery with a sandboxed hosted instance + synthetic data to prove latency/cost before Private deployment talks.
- Keep the pilot narrowly scoped: agent assist template generation + 1 routing flow is enough to show impact.

End of scratchpad.

---
Revision log: 2024-11-03 initial draft by Ethan; 2024-11-12 added discovery checklist and pilot options; 2024-11-20 updated outreach templates and added linked artifacts.

- Cost controls: batching/prefix caching reduce token spend (pull numbers in discovery).
- Rollout-friendly: can start hosted dev instance -> Dedicated pilot -> Private if they need on-prem.

Initial outreach plan (sequence):
1) Warm intros: check LinkedIn 1st-degree overlaps (Maya + Rafael).
2) Email 1 (short value + ask for 20m): subject variants below.
3) LinkedIn InMail if no response after 4 days.
4) Follow-up with one-pager + pilot terms after interest.

Email drafts (lightweight, pick tone per persona):
- For Head of Digital (Sana):
Subject: Quick pilot idea to cut contact center handle time by ~20%
Hi Sana — quick note from Ethan at Redwood Inference. We help banks run production-grade LLMs (hosted, dedicated, or private) focused on predictable latency and cost.
I read HavenBank’s digital transformation piece and thought there’s a low-risk pilot we could run to: 1) reduce AHT for chat/phone by ~15–25% and 2) keep PII inside your environment via our Private deployment.
Could we grab 20 minutes to compare notes and I’ll show a 7-slide pilot plan?
Best,
Ethan

- For Dir of Security (Leah):
Subject: PII, KMS, and auditability for LLM pilots
Leah — quick intro: we’ve run Private deployments for regulated customers with customer KMS/HSM integration, full audit logs, and egress controls. Happy to share an architecture note and threat model. 10–15m?

LinkedIn InMail short template:
Hi Leah — Ethan from Redwood Inference. We do private LLM deployments with customer KMS and SOC2-compatible audit trails. Would you be open to a 15m call for a short architecture sketch?

Discovery checklist (what to learn on first call):
- Traffic volume (monthly, peak concurrent chats, average tokens per session)
- Current AHT for channels (phone, chat, email). Baseline metrics.
- What data stays in-bank vs. third-party? Any data that cannot leave VPC?
- Who owns procurement/infra approval? Expected procurement timeline.
- Existing monitoring: do they have per-route latency dashboards? any SRE on-call constraints for pilot?
- Success criteria for pilot (ops vs. cost vs. accuracy).
- Technical contacts: infra lead, security, and sample PII flows to redact/transform.

Pilot scope options (fast to implement):
A) Agent assist + canned KYC template generation (4–6 weeks)
- 1 call center team (50 agents) traffic mirror -> evaluate AHT and handoff quality.
- Provide SDK + small on-prem model or dedicated instance behind VPC.
- Success: 15% drop in AHT, >90% agent satisfaction from NPS survey.
B) Customer-facing chat pilot (6–8 weeks)
- Start with non-sensitive FAQs + product lookup.
- Use routing policy -> fallback to smaller local model for low-latency.
- Success: improved containment rate, reduce escalations by 10%.

Quick technical approach (for conversations with platform/security):
- Option 1: Private deployment (control plane in Redwood, data plane in customer VPC) — KMS/HSM integration, no egress of raw transcripts.
- Option 2: Dedicated instance in AWS account with strict IAM roles and VPC endpoints.
- Kernel/config considerations: choose quantized LLM variant for cost; enable KV caching for repeated phrases (e.g., account-number prompts) to reduce token cost.
- Observability: token-level cost and latency dashboards per-route, audit logs streaming to Splunk/ELK, trace IDs for every request.

Objection handling (common):
- "We can’t send PII to a 3rd party": -> Offer Private deployment + bring-your-own-KMS and an architecture review + SOC2 package + contractual data protection.
- "LLMs make mistakes / hallucinate": -> Use retrieval augmented generation for facts (customer data read-only connectors), quality gates, and human-in-loop fallback policies. Show evals: precision on KYC template extraction >92% on a small labeled set.
- "Cost unknown / token bill": -> Walk through expected token profile, show batching & caching recommendations; propose a fixed-price pilot tranche to remove variable spend risk.

Numbers to have pre-call (ballpark):
- Token estimate example: 10k sessions/month x 250 tokens/session = 2.5M tokens/month. With caching/batching reduce to ~1.7M tokens. At hosted list price ~ $X/token (redacted in external copy) => pilot cost estimate $Y (present on call).
- Latency targets: live agent assist aim: p95 < 250ms for LLM completion after caching; if not achievable, aim for <400ms with fallback.

Pilot legal & procurement touchpoints:
- Need to know legal standard SLAs, privacy addenda, and whether they require data processing agreement + DPA + DPAs for sub-processors.
- Procurement timeline: aim for 4 weeks for pilot SOW signoff if there are no major compliance blockers.

Call outline (15–20m intro call):
1) 2m intros + why I reached out
2) 4–6m high-level pilot idea + quick ROI sketch
3) 6–8m discovery questions (use checklist)
4) 2–3m next steps: tech follow-up, security docs, pilot SOW

Interactive notes / quick things to test on POC:
- Synthetic PCI/PII masking module — test if they can accept redacted inputs.
- KV cache behavior on repeated billing questions (should be near-zero extra tokens).
- Failure-mode test: simulate model fallback -> verify routing + tracing works.

Open questions for internal team:
- Who on Solutions Eng can lead a 2-week full-immersion engineering spike? (Maya indicated availability after 11/15).
- Do we have a pre-made pilot SOW template for regional banks (I think there is one in CONFLUENCE-Redwood-embed-playbook-314)?
- Pricing: propose fixed pilot cap (suggest $15k–25k depending on scope) — confirm with pricing ops.

Next actions (this doc):
- [ ] Run quick lookup for 1st-degree LinkedIn overlaps (Maya)
- [ ] Draft email to Sana + Leah + Mark (Ethan) by 11/05
- [ ] Pull baseline AHT industry benchmarks for regional banks (Rafael)
- [ ] Prepare brief security summary (2 slides) for Leah before call (Maya)

Notes-to-self / spin ideas:
- If they push for lower risk path: propose a 2-week discovery with a sandboxed hosted instance + synthetic data to prove latency/cost before Private deployment talks.
- Keep the pilot narrowly scoped: agent assist template generation + 1 routing flow is enough to show impact.

End of scratchpad.

---
Revision log: 2024-11-03 initial draft by Ethan; 2024-11-12 added discovery checklist and pilot options; 2024-11-20 updated outreach templates and added linked artifacts.

source.title: Shadow cohort spinup and rollback simulation notes
source.content:
Purpose:
Quick personal scratchpad for running a shadow cohort (mirror traffic) and rehearsing rollback scenarios before we flip any live routing. Not an org playbook — more a bootstrapping checklist + gotchas list I use to run sims and hand off to on-call/owner.

Context and when to use this:
- Use when rolling a new model binary or new runtime change that *touches hotpath* (KV layout, attention kernel, cache eviction).
- When we want to validate upstream production traffic without exposing users to output changes (mirror-only) AND validate rollback automation works (dry-run + simulated failure).

Goals for the run:
- Validate that shaded/mirrored requests don't increase p99 latency above baseline by > 10% under typical load.
- Confirm rollback automation (traffic reconfiguration + unpin) completes within SLO (target: 90s for config flip + 5m to stabilize).
- Prove pinning/unpin TTL behavior for client SDKs (no stale affinity after rollback).
- Surface hidden error modes: silent truncation, token corruption, KV cache desync.

Quick checklist (order-of-ops)
1) Preflight (30–60m before experiment window)
   - Confirm canary image/model is built and SHAed (commit -> chash map ready).
   - Create a shadow cohort name and manifest: shadow-cohort/<date>/<owner>.
   - Approve capacity reservation in Dedicated console (if dedicated) or check hosted quotas.
   - Smoke test deploy in dev with small seeded requests that exercise cache warm path.
   - Update runbook link and slack thread; @oncall notified.

2) Spinup (0–15m)
   - Start service with environment tag: SHADOW=true, MIRROR_RATE=100% (mirror mode), CLIENT_BIND_TTL=30s (short for test).
   - Enable traffic mirroring in routing policy: mirror only, no impact to production responses.
   - Attach lightweight tracing and new trace sampling for shadow requests (trace id suffix .shadow).
\   - Kick off warmup: send 2k synthetic requests across common prompt shapes to populate KV caches.

3) Observe (15–90m)
   - Watch metrics: p50/p95/p99 latency, error count, 4xx/5xx breakdown, token-gen rate, model-heuristic-regressions (similarity or hallucination spike detections).
   - Cost watch: estimate incremental GPU-mins and token costs for run. NOTE: if cost > $X/hr, pull caps.
   - Check consistency: sample 1% of mirrored pairs and run lightweight diff to detect structural output divergence (not content quality) — e.g., schema differences, missing function calls.

4) Rollback simulation (rehearse)
   - Execute scripted rollback in dry-run mode first (no config changes) to measure timing of each step. Capture output.
   - Simulate failure: create a weighted failure by toggling unhealthy healthcheck for the shadow nodes. Observe that rollback automation would pick them up (but keep mirroring live).
   - Run full rollback once with traffic split flip to fallback model (or old runtime) and confirm client pins are released.
   - Confirm post-rollback baseline metrics return to pre-run values within 10m.

5) Post-run (15–60m)
   - Capture a short postmortem note in the release thread with: what we tested, duration, observed regressions, timeline, owner, next steps.
   - If any pinned clients remained bound > TTL, open INF ticket and mark as blocker.

Commands and snippets I use
- Spinup (k8s example): kubectl apply -f shadow-deploy.yaml --record && kubectl set env deploy/shadow-deploy SHADOW=true MIRROR_RATE=100 CLIENT_BIND_TTL=30
- Flip routing (feature flag): rwctl route set mirror=false activeModel=stable-v2 fallbackModel=stable-v1 --reason "rollback-sim"
- Rollback simulation (dry-run): rwctl rollout simulate --target shadow-cohort --dry-run
- Collect logs (filtered): rwl logs --since 10m --selector cohort=shadow-20241103 | grep "trace.*\.shadow" > /tmp/shadow-traces.log

Acceptance criteria (canary dry-run)
- Latency: p99_shadow < 1.1 * p99_prod OR absolute increase < 75ms
- Errors: shadow_5xx_rate < 2x prod_5xx_rate AND no sustained 5xx spike > 5m
- Functional: no schema-level diffs for 95% of sampled mirrored responses
- Pin/unpin: client-binding TTL observed to expire < configured + 5s in 95% clients

Edge cases and gotchas I've hit before
- Mirror amplification: if mirrors auto-retry, you can accidentally double-count and overload the shadow pool. Ensure mirror requests carry idempotency key and disable client retries for mirrored path.
- KV cache warm: some models require ~50 unique prefixes before stable latency. Warmup scripts need to mimic real request distribution, not just one template.
- Hidden truncation: look at token delta between mirrored pairs — silent early-stop is easy to miss if only checking status codes.
- Pin stickiness: certain SDK versions cached binding locally; ensure test clients use the latest SDK or manually clear client-side cache.

Open questions / TODOs
- Decide: default client_bind_ttl for shadow runs (30s?) vs prod runs (5m). Pros/cons for shorter TTL: faster recovery vs noise in affinity metrics.
- Automate the sample-diffing pipeline so we don't eyeball outputs. Placeholder: small lambda to compute structural diffs and push results to BigQuery.
- Make rollback-sim a periodic chaos run (once per sprint) and add to team calendar.

Handover checklist for oncall after I run this
- Link to the run thread and artifacts (logs, smoke report).
- What to watch in the first 15m post-flip: p99, error spike, client complaints (#support mentions).
- Who to call: Maya (runtime lead), Zoe (SRE), Priya (CS for high-impact clients).

One-off notes:
- If we see hallucination changes in mirrored responses, tag them with "quality:regression" and attach 5 examples to the eval dashboard.
- For Dedicated customers, coordinate with infra to ensure we don't accidentally preempt committed concurrency.

Last run memory (2024-09-21):
- Warmup insufficient -> p99 spike at minute 3. Fix: add templated prompt diversity and increase warmup RPS 2x.
- Client-bind TTL bug: older SDKs kept TTL cached for 10m; bumped TTL and cleared cache on clients.

End notes:
This is my quick personal checklist I carry into any shadow cohort or mirror-based canary. Keep it short, run it, iterate on automation where it hurts most (diffing and rollback timing measurement).

Questions I want to ask before next run:
- Can we add a routed indicator in the trace UI to mark mirrored traces separately (so they don't pollute prod sampling)?
- Can the Dedicated console show an "estimated incremental GPU minutes" before spinup?

-- Ethan

   - If any pinned clients remained bound > TTL, open INF ticket and mark as blocker.

Commands and snippets I use
- Spinup (k8s example): kubectl apply -f shadow-deploy.yaml --record && kubectl set env deploy/shadow-deploy SHADOW=true MIRROR_RATE=100 CLIENT_BIND_TTL=30
- Flip routing (feature flag): rwctl route set mirror=false activeModel=stable-v2 fallbackModel=stable-v1 --reason "rollback-sim"
- Rollback simulation (dry-run): rwctl rollout simulate --target shadow-cohort --dry-run
- Collect logs (filtered): rwl logs --since 10m --selector cohort=shadow-20241103 | grep "trace.*\.shadow" > /tmp/shadow-traces.log

Acceptance criteria (canary dry-run)
- Latency: p99_shadow < 1.1 * p99_prod OR absolute increase < 75ms
- Errors: shadow_5xx_rate < 2x prod_5xx_rate AND no sustained 5xx spike > 5m
- Functional: no schema-level diffs for 95% of sampled mirrored responses
- Pin/unpin: client-binding TTL observed to expire < configured + 5s in 95% clients

Edge cases and gotchas I've hit before
- Mirror amplification: if mirrors auto-retry, you can accidentally double-count and overload the shadow pool. Ensure mirror requests carry idempotency key and disable client retries for mirrored path.
- KV cache warm: some models require ~50 unique prefixes before stable latency. Warmup scripts need to mimic real request distribution, not just one template.
- Hidden truncation: look at token delta between mirrored pairs — silent early-stop is easy to miss if only checking status codes.
- Pin stickiness: certain SDK versions cached binding locally; ensure test clients use the latest SDK or manually clear client-side cache.

Open questions / TODOs
- Decide: default client_bind_ttl for shadow runs (30s?) vs prod runs (5m). Pros/cons for shorter TTL: faster recovery vs noise in affinity metrics.
- Automate the sample-diffing pipeline so we don't eyeball outputs. Placeholder: small lambda to compute structural diffs and push results to BigQuery.
- Make rollback-sim a periodic chaos run (once per sprint) and add to team calendar.

Handover checklist for oncall after I run this
- Link to the run thread and artifacts (logs, smoke report).
- What to watch in the first 15m post-flip: p99, error spike, client complaints (#support mentions).
- Who to call: Maya (runtime lead), Zoe (SRE), Priya (CS for high-impact clients).

One-off notes:
- If we see hallucination changes in mirrored responses, tag them with "quality:regression" and attach 5 examples to the eval dashboard.
- For Dedicated customers, coordinate with infra to ensure we don't accidentally preempt committed concurrency.

Last run memory (2024-09-21):
- Warmup insufficient -> p99 spike at minute 3. Fix: add templated prompt diversity and increase warmup RPS 2x.
- Client-bind TTL bug: older SDKs kept TTL cached for 10m; bumped TTL and cleared cache on clients.

End notes:
This is my quick personal checklist I carry into any shadow cohort or mirror-based canary. Keep it short, run it, iterate on automation where it hurts most (diffing and rollback timing measurement).

Questions I want to ask before next run:
- Can we add a routed indicator in the trace UI to mark mirrored traces separately (so they don't pollute prod sampling)?
- Can the Dedicated console show an "estimated incremental GPU minutes" before spinup?

-- Ethan

source.title: Stream scratchpad thrashing and CUDA workspace sizing notes
source.content:
Summary:

This note captures debugging iterations and hypotheses for a recurring class of OOM and high tail-latency incidents we observed in streaming/scoring workloads on multi-stream GPUs. Symptoms: intermittent OOMs during steady-state traffic (not growth spikes), repeatable higher-than-expected peak resident memory, and long pauses in request completion correlated with cudaMalloc/cudaFree and kernel scheduling stalls.

High-level hypothesis (current):
- Workloads that use many short-lived ephemeral buffers (per-request scratch/workspace) create high allocator churn across CUDA streams. This churn combines with large library (cuBLAS/cuDNN) temporary workspace allocations to fragment internal arenas and force slow cudaMalloc/cudaFree paths.
- In particular, convolution/cuBLAS workspace size calls can vary by invocation (shape/algorithm choice) and may request transient allocations that don't fit existing freelists, causing synchronous device allocations and long stalls.
- Dynamic batching and variable-length inputs exacerbate the issue by rapidly changing peak allocation sets and by preventing stable reuse patterns.

Key observations from traces and experiments:

1) Timeline correlation (nsight/cupti traces)
- When tail latency spikes occur, there is a burst of cudaMalloc / cudaFree pairs on multiple streams for buffers in the 64KB-8MB range. These bursts align with the sampling of convolution algorithm selection and with non-deterministic fallback to slower algos.
- GPU-side traces show periods where kernels are queued but not running while cudaMalloc is executing on other streams and the device allocator is contending. This indicates device-level allocation stalls are impacting scheduling fairness.

2) Host-side allocator behavior (cudamallocasync vs legacy)
- Moving to cudaMallocAsync with stream-ordered free semantics reduced the incidence of global stalls but did not eliminate OOMs. Async allocator improved peak throughput under synthetic steady patterns but still struggled with bursty, highly variable transient allocations.
- With legacy cudaMalloc, we saw longer pauses (1–50 ms) during high-churn windows; the async allocator reduced the long tail but increased fragmentation unless retains were tuned.

3) Library workspace sizing interaction
- cuDNN/cuBLAS workspace size queries return algorithm-dependent sizes. When the runtime queries for best algos per-batch, it sometimes picks a memory-hungry implementation for one invocation then a smaller one for the next, producing temporary overshoot above the freelist bins.
- For some models we saw repeated calls to getWorkspaceSize where the reported size fluctuated by >2x between similar batches (likely due to subtle input shape or alignment differences).

4) Dynamic batching and padding patterns
- Variable sequence lengths cause transient spikes: a long sequence in the batch forces internal KV cache or attention workspace growth; when that request completes its scratch is freed but memory becomes fragmented, making following allocations fail even though total free memory could hold the new allocation if contiguous.
- Our autotuned batcher occasionally coalesces heterogeneous inputs producing buffer sets that are hard to reuse between iterations.

Reproduction recipe (local / CI):
- Use a multi-stream workload generator that: (a) emits requests with 3-4 discrete length classes (short/medium/long), (b) artificially randomizes batch composition, and (c) toggles algorithm selection via environment (set deterministic cudnn heuristic off/on).
- Run on a 16GB GPU VM with TORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=garbage_collection_threshold:0.6,max_split_size_mb:128 and collect: nvprof/NSys trace + cudaMemGetInfo sampled at 1s + cupti kernel/allocator markers.
- Expected: reproduce OOM or high tail when allocator retain settings are low and cudnn chooses large algos.

Quick telemetry queries we ran:
- db: request_events where outcome in (OOM,error) group by model, shape, batch_size, algo_choice -> shows concentration on two models and the 2 longest-length classes.
- gpu_mem_snapshots: max_used_mb, free_mb over sliding 60s windows aligned to request start -> pronounced post-request fragmentation dips.

Mitigations tried and results:
- Preallocated per-stream scratch pools:
  - Strategy: allocate a pool of fixed-size scratch arenas per stream at model load time; satisfy workspace requests from those pools.
  - Result: significantly reduced cudaMalloc/cudaFree calls and removed most long pauses. However, if pool sizing underestimated peak per-request needs, we still saw OOMs. Pools reduce churn but require careful sizing per-model.

- Pessimistic algorithm choice / cap workspace size:
  - We force cudnn to choose memory-conservative algos or cap the workspace size reported to the runtime.
  - Result: lowered peak memory and eliminated some OOM cases at cost of slower kernels (~5-20% perf regression). Useful as automated fallback when allocation pressure detected.

- Tune cudaMallocAsync retains and max_split_size_mb (CUDA 11/12):
  - Increasing retain and max_split_size_mb reduced fragmentation in mixed-size alloc patterns but increased overall resident set and hence base memory usage.
  - There's a sweet spot per-GPU-model; defaults are not good for streaming patterns.

- Batch composition constraints / padding harmonization:
  - Enforce stricter batch bucketing to avoid mixing very long and short sequences in the same batch. This reduced peak allocation variance and improved reuse. Workload-dependent but low-friction at the router layer.

- Rate-limiting and backpressure at ingress:
  - Smoothing bursty arrival patterns prevents transient oversubscription of scratch arenas and library workspaces. This is an operational mitigation rather than a runtime patch.

Open hypotheses and next experiments:
- Investigate NUMA and host pinned memory behavior: some allocations are falling back to pinned host staging leading to host-side stalls. Run experiments toggling CUDA_PIN_MEMORY and monitoring host memcpy latency in traces.
- Probe algorithm selection determinism: instrument cudnnGetConvolution* in the runtime to log returned algos and workspace sizes for every batch; correlate with shapes and alignment to see what triggers large-workspace algos.
- Explore hybrid strategy: prefer small-memory algos for first N inferences, then switch to high-throughput algos once reuse pattern stabilizes (heuristic per model/endpoint).
- For cudaMallocAsync, experiment with deliberate per-stream arenas with slab sizing tuned by p95 observed allocation size instead of global bucket heuristics.

Operational recommendations (short-term):
- Add a circuit-breaker: if device free memory dips below threshold AND fragmentation metric (max_free_contiguous_mb / total_free_mb) low, immediately restrict algos (memory-conservative) and slow down ingress.
- Add an observability dashboard for: per-stream allocation rate, per-allocation-size histogram, cudaMalloc latency histogram, cudnn workspace sizes per inference. This will make root cause faster to detect in future incidents.
- For high-throughput production clusters, configure CUDA allocator retains and max_split_size_mb as part of image defaults tuned to the GPU type and workload class.

Appendix: quick commands and trace tips
- NSYS capture focusing on allocation kernels: nsys profile --trace=cuda,nvtx --output=alloc_trace --sample=none --capture-range=nvtx --stop-on-exit true <run>
- Small script to sample cudaMemGetInfo every 250ms and annotate traces with free/total memory (useful for correlating allocation spikes).
- Use cudaMallocAsync + stream-ordered deallocation for stream-local transient buffers; avoid cross-stream frees unless necessary.

Notes for reviewers:
- This doc collates several WIP branches and profiling runs; intended outcome is to converge to a sustainable policy for per-model workspace sizing and allocator defaults.
- Prioritize: (1) observability (allocation histograms), (2) runtime circuit-breaker, (3) prototype per-stream scratch arenas for the two affected models.

Action items:
- [Ethan] instrument cudnn workspace queries and push loggable hook (PR linked).
- [María] run pinned-memory vs unpinned experiments and post traces to the thread.
- [Li] prototype per-stream slab allocator variant in serving runtime and measure p95/p99 improvement.
- [Connor] add dashboard tiles for allocator metrics and a floor/threshold alert.

End of note.
