source.title: Aegis Pharma — integration thoughtstream (private deploy)
source.content:
Context:
Quick working notes from discovery / kickoff with Aegis Pharma (R&D informatics). They are exploring a private deployment of Redwood for hallucination-sensitive summarization of clinical trial notes and protocol ingestion. High sensitivity: PHI in unstructured notes, strict auditability, need for on-prem or VPC-isolated control plane with KMS/HSM integration. This doc is a free-form thoughtstream — not polished.

Call recap (2024-09-15):
- Attendees: Ethan (Redwood SE), Sofia (Redwood Solutions), Priya (Redwood PM), Dr. Martin Chen (Aegis Data Lead), Rana Kapoor (Aegis SecOps), Alejandro Ruiz (Aegis Platform).
- Duration: ~60m. High-level ask: private deployment option that can run nightly batch summarization and real-time triage assistant for safety team.
- Immediate goal: small proof-of-concept (PoC) to validate latency and PHI handling.

Notes & raw observations (bullet-y, messy):
- Two workloads: 1) batch summarization of ~5000 docs/day (avg 2k tokens, peak 8k), 2) near-real-time triage for incoming safety reports (p50 ~120ms token latency target for short prompts, p95 < 600ms).
- They want model determinism for audits — deterministic seeds per run are OK but need reproducible prompts and pinned model versions.
- Compliance: HIPAA + internal retention policy (audit logs for 7 years). Need proof that audit logs are tamper-evident; they pressed on key-wrap/HSM for KMS.
- Network constraints: their on-prem network prohibits outbound calls to public internet for PHI. VPC option with customer-managed control plane is preferred; if we propose private control plane, confirm egress rules and whether any telemetry can be allowed.
- Data flow sketch (rough): EHR -> ETL worker (sanitizer) -> Redwood Private endpoint (VPC) -> model -> results written to internal store + audit log. Must show where we're removing/transforming PHI vs leaving raw text.
- Ops: Aegis wants runbooks for failure modes (fallback to smaller model, circuit-breaker), and SLA for model latency for triage path. They are used to internal SRE style runbooks, want similar docs from us.
- Model selection: they asked about smaller LLMs fine-tuned on medical corpora. We suggested redwood-med-7B-quant (example) as candidate for PoC with fallback to hosted redwood-med-13B (non-PHI path) for higher quality if allowed. Need to document quality vs cost tradeoffs.

Gotchas / risk flags to validate quickly:
- KMS/HSM: integration pattern matters. Customer key (you manage key in HSM) vs Redwood-managed KM: They require HSM with key material never leaving their boundary. Need architecture diagram for CMK import/wrap.
- Audit retention: retention and immutability. If customer runs on their infra, they expect logs to live in their SIEM. If we run control plane, they want proof that logs are pushed to their collector.
- Network egress policy might force a full on-prem offline packaging exercise (air-gapped). That shifts effort estimate by 3-4x vs VPC. Confirm which option they want after PoC.
- Latency SLOs may be tight on older GPU inventory. Need realistic perf testing: sequence length distribution for triage is short (<=256 tokens), but summarization long. Batching strategies differ.
- Tokenization & PII redaction: they want a pre-checker step to remove phone numbers, SSNs. We should recommend a lightweight regex + ML PII classifier run locally before sending to model. Document will include suggested open-source components.

Immediate technical questions (need answers before engineering estimate):
1) Which infra option? (VPC-private-control-plane OR fully on-prem air-gapped package) — Rana to confirm by 2024-09-22.
2) Do they require HSM with keys that never leave their HSM? If yes, confirm vendor (AWS CloudHSM vs Thales vs other) and whether they will accept a key-wrap flow.
3) Expected SLA/uptime window for triage path (business hours only?) and RTO for batch pipeline.
4) Can non-PHI telemetry be sent to Redwood for observability, or do they forbid all external telemetry?
5) Dataset size for fine-tune / eval (how many labeled docs for supervision)? Dr. Chen to share sample set (anonymized) by next week.

Draft PoC plan (very rough):
- Phase 0 (1 week): confirm infra option + security signoffs; exchange non-prod network diagrams.
- Phase 1 (2-3 weeks): deploy private control plane in Aegis VPC, provision dedicated inference nodes (small), integrate KMS key-wrap, run against synthetic sanitized data for perf baselining.

title: Aegis Pharma — integration thoughtstream (private deploy)
preview:
Context:
Quick working notes from discovery / kickoff with Aegis Pharma (R&D informatics). They are exploring a private deployment of Redwood for hallucination-sensitive summarization of clinical trial notes and protocol ingestion. High sensitivity: PHI in unstructured notes, strict auditability, need for on-prem or VPC-isolated control plane with KMS/HSM integration. This doc is a free-form thoughtstream — not polished.

Call recap (2024-09-15):
- Attendees: Ethan (Redwood SE), Sofia (Redwood Solutions), Priya (Redwood PM), Dr. Martin Chen (Aegis Data Lead), Rana Kapoor (Aegis SecOps), Alejandro Ruiz (Aegis Platform).
- Duration: ~60m. High-level ask: private deployment option that can run nightly batch summarization and real-time triage assistant for safety team.
- Immediate goal: small proof-of-concept (PoC) to validate latency and PHI handling.

Notes & raw observations (bullet-y, messy):
- Two workloads: 1) batch summarization of ~5000 docs/day (avg 2k tokens, peak 8k), 2) near-real-time triage for incoming safety reports (p50 ~120ms token latency target for short prompts, p95 < 600ms).
- They want model determinism for audits — deterministic seeds per run are OK but need reproducible prompts and pinned model versions.
- Compliance: HIPAA + internal retention policy (audit logs for 7 years). Need proof that audit logs are tamper-evident; they pressed on key-wrap/HSM for KMS.
- Network constraints: their on-prem network prohibits outbound calls to public internet for PHI. VPC option with customer-managed control plane is preferred; if we propose private control plane, confirm egress rules and whether any telemetry can be allowed.
- Data flow sketch (rough): EHR -> ETL worker (sanitizer) -> Redwood Private endpoint (VPC) -> model -> results written to internal store + audit log. Must show where we're removing/transforming PHI vs leaving raw text.
- Ops: Aegis wants runbooks for failure modes (fallback to smaller model, circuit-breaker), and SLA for model latency for triage path. They are used to internal SRE style runbooks, want similar docs from us.
- Model selection: they asked about smaller LLMs fine-tuned on medical corpora. We suggested redwood-med-7B-quant (example) as candidate for PoC with fallback to hosted redwood-med-13B (non-PHI path) for higher quality if allowed. Need to document quality vs cost tradeoffs.

Gotchas / risk flags to validate quickly:
- KMS/HSM: integration pattern matters. Customer key (you manage key in HSM) vs Redwood-managed KM: They require HSM with key material never leaving their boundary. Need architecture diagram for CMK import/wrap.
- Audit retention: retention and immutability. If customer runs on their infra, they expect logs to live in their SIEM. If we run control plane, they want proof that logs are pushed to their collector.
- Network egress policy might force a full on-prem offline packaging exercise (air-gapped). That shifts effort estimate by 3-4x vs VPC. Confirm which option they want after PoC.
- Latency SLOs may be tight on older GPU inventory. Need realistic perf testing: sequence length distribution for triage is short (<=256 tokens), but summarization long. Batching strategies differ.
- Tokenization & PII redaction: they want a pre-checker step to remove phone numbers, SSNs. We should recommend a lightweight regex + ML PII classifier run locally before sending to model. Document will include suggested open-source components.

Immediate technical questions (need answers before engineering estimate):
1) Which infra option? (VPC-private-control-plane OR fully on-prem air-gapped package) — Rana to confirm by 2024-09-22.
2) Do they require HSM with keys that never leave their HSM? If yes, confirm vendor (AWS CloudHSM vs Thales vs other) and whether they will accept a key-wrap flow.
3) Expected SLA/uptime window for triage path (business hours only?) and RTO for batch pipeline.
4) Can non-PHI telemetry be sent to Redwood for observability, or do they forbid all external telemetry?
5) Dataset size for fine-tune / eval (how many labeled docs for supervision)? Dr. Chen to share sample set (anonymized) by next week.

Draft PoC plan (very rough):
- Phase 0 (1 week): confirm infra option + security signoffs; exchange non-prod network diagrams.
- Phase 1 (2-3 weeks): deploy private control plane in Aegis VPC, provision dedicated inference nodes (small), integrate KMS key-wrap, run against synthetic sanitized data for perf baselining.
- Phase 2 (3-4 weeks): run batch summarization job, instrument audit logs to SIEM, iterate PII preprocessor. Evaluate quality (precision/recall on clinical entities) vs latency/cost.
- Phase 3 (2 weeks): run small canary of triage assistant with limited users, refine SLOs and runbooks.

Engineering notes / hints for follow-up:
- For triage path, suggest a 2-tier routing policy: low-latency pool (smaller quantized model) and high-quality fallback (larger model) with automatic fallback on token budget or latency.
- Batching: real-time path should disable aggressive batching and rely on single-request fast path. For nightly batch, use throughput-optimized batching with KV cache warmers.
- Quantization: recommend 4-bit quant for med-7B for PoC; check quality degradation on entity extraction tasks.
- Auditing: ensure every request has a unique request_id, include model_version, prompt_hash, and kcv (key-check-value) for the KMS invocation in the audit event.
- Secrets & KMS: We may need to provide example HSM integration code snippets; flag to infra team.

Open action items (owner, due):
- Rana (Aegis) -> confirm infra option (VPC vs air-gap) by 2024-09-22.
- Ethan (me) -> produce short architecture sketch + three deployment options (VPC private control plane, dedicated-hosted, air-gapped on-prem bundle). Due 2024-09-20. (blocked on security checklist template from Priya).
- Sofia -> draft KMS integration pattern doc and example flows (key-wrap vs remote-provide). Due 2024-09-27.
- Priya -> provide example runbook template and previous SRE-runbook sample. Due 2024-09-24.

source.title: Routing Tenancy Decision Checklist
source.content:
Purpose:
This is a practical decision checklist and set of annotated scenarios to help platform engineers choose routing and isolation defaults across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments. It focuses on: isolation boundaries, tenant routing semantics, control-plane responsibilities, multi-region failover, and operational runbook tasks for onboarding and incident response.

Intended audience:
Platform engineers, SREs, solutions engineers, and security reviewers who make trade-offs between latency, cost, compliance, and reliability when selecting routing models for a customer or workload.

High-level decision flow (one-page):
1) Ask: Does the customer require physical compute isolation or just logical isolation?
   - Physical isolation OR dedicated capacity for throughput/SLA -> Consider Dedicated or Private.
   - Logical isolation (per-tenant namespaces, separate creds) acceptable -> Hosted is possible.
2) Ask: Are there egress or data residency constraints?
   - Yes -> Private (VPC/on-prem) or Dedicated with region pinning.
3) Ask: Does the workload need predictable latency at scale?
   - Tight 99th latency targets with steady traffic -> Dedicated capacity pools.
   - Bursty/flexible -> Hosted with autoscale + burst policies.
4) Ask: Can control plane be centralized?
   - For Private (air-gapped), control plane must be optional local or federated.
   - For Hosted/Dedicated, central control plane with regional zones is default.

Checklist: configuration knobs to choose per deployment mode
- Tenant identity surface: API key only vs. API key + tenant-id headers. Default: all modes use tenant-id header.
- Routing affinity: region affinity vs. stateless routing. Default: Hosted -> region-aware but stateless; Dedicated -> affinity to capacity pool; Private -> customer-defined affinity.
- Egress footprint: shared NAT vs. per-tenant egress IPs. Default: Hosted -> shared; Dedicated/Private -> per-tenant or per-commit egress.
- KV/prefix caching: global shared cache vs. tenant-scoped cache. Default: isolate tokenized caches per-tenant for privacy, share read-only model artifacts.
- Fallback policy: model-variant fallback (cheaper/quantized) vs. capacity-tier fallback. Default: Hosted -> automatic fallback; Dedicated -> controlled fallback (owner approval); Private -> disabled unless explicitly configured.

Isolation boundary patterns (concrete examples)
- Logical-only isolation (Hosted, default):
  - Single serving runtime clusters per region.
  - Namespaces and RBAC isolate metadata, but compute and kernels are shared.
  - Advantages: cost efficiency, fast routing, easier autoscaling.
  - Risks: noisy neighbor; require strong telemetry + dynamic throttles.

- Tenant pool isolation (Dedicated):
  - Dedicated GPU pools pinned to customers or groups.
  - Control plane routes by pool-id, runtime enforces hard tenant fence (cgroups, container-level).
  - Advantages: predictable throughput, easier per-tenant SLA.
  - Risks: underutilization unless burst/overflow patterns allowed.

- Full private isolation (Private/VPC or on-prem):
  - Customer controls compute/networking. Control plane either federated or local agent manages runtime.
  - Service-level routing occurs inside customer's network; Redwood control plane issues manifests.
  - Advantages: compliance, full egress control.
  - Risks: operational overhead, slower feature rollout.

Control plane responsibilities by mode
- Hosted control plane (centralized):
  - Global model catalog, billing, usage analytics, routing policies, and rollout control.
  - Maintains regional proxies for latency-sensitive routing decisions.
  - Responsible for automatic fallback rules and cross-region reroute.

- Dedicated control plane (hybrid):
  - Central control plane owns policy + telemetry; local control-plane agents own capacity placement and low-level routing within dedicated pools.
  - Policy enforcement: central policy evaluates tenant eligibility for pool; local agent enforces affinity and local failover.

- Private control plane (federated/local):
  - Minimal central visibility (billing/health ping optional).
  - Local control plane must support model pinning, security policy enforcement, and optional sync of audit logs to Redwood.

Routing policies & examples (pseudo rules)
- Policy: region-affinity-with-fallback
  - rule: prefer region == tenant.preferred_region; if capacity < 80% then route to nearest-region with compatible model; if latency projection > SLO then fallback to compressed-variant.
- Policy: strict-isolation
  - rule: only route to pools tagged tenant_id == X and do not use shared caches; reject if no available capacity.
- Policy: cost-saver
  - rule: route to quantized variant during off-peak hours unless quality degradation alert triggers.

Multi-region failover matrix (short)
- Hosted: cross-region routing enabled by default; failover latency includes control-plane decision + regional proxy hop. SLA: best-effort regional failover within 30s for degraded regions.
- Dedicated: failover within same commit/tenant pool family; if failover to different pool required, apply canary validation for 5–10 minutes.
- Private: failover decisions are customer-configured; Redwood recommends a primary/secondary region manifest with health probes.

Telemetry and tagging requirements (must-have for safe shared-hosted)
- Token-level cost attribution tags (model, quantization, route)
- Per-request tenancy tag (tenant_id, pool_id, region)
- Noise detection: continuous scoring of per-tenant token variance and latency spikes to trigger throttles or suggested pool move

Operational runbook (onboard-to-live in 6 steps)
1) Customer intake questionnaire (chooses isolation level, region, egress, compliance needs).
2) Default routing profile generation (we assign region affinity + fallback).
3) Capacity plan and cost estimation (if Dedicated, reserve N GPUs or autoscale schedule).
4) Local/networking config (for Private: VPC peering, NAT egress mapping, control-plane agent install).
5) Smoke tests: end-to-end latency, region-failover drill, egress verification, and model-variant degradation check.
6) Production cutover with canary: start at 5% traffic for 1 hour, validate telemetry, accelerate to 100% if green.

Incident playbook highlights (routing-related incidents)
- Symptom: wide latency spike in a region
  - Immediately: instantiate temporary cross-region routing override to nearest healthy region, toggle autoscaling up for affected pools.
  - If owned pool: move affected tenants to secondary dedicated pool; notify CS and tenant.
- Symptom: noisy tenant consuming disproportionate tokens
  - Immediately: apply dynamic throttling, reduce max concurrency, open support ticket with tenant.
- Symptom: egress IP requirement violated
  - Immediately: rollback to previous routing manifest, adjust NAT mapping; for Private, coordinate with customer networking.

Trade-offs and recommended defaults (summary)
- Default for SMB/no-compliance: Hosted with region affinity, automatic fallback, shared egress, and per-tenant logical isolation.
- Default for high-throughput/SLA customers: Dedicated with tenant pool isolation, per-tenant egress, and controlled fallback policies.
- Default for regulated/data-residency: Private with federated control plane and customer-managed routing.

Open questions to resolve (for engineering roadmap)
- What is the exact API surface for tenant-level local routing hints? (low-latency header vs control-plane manifest?)
- How to represent multi-tenant cache sharing in telemetry without leaking PII?
- Run cost simulation tool: need a calculator to show Dedicated idle vs burst economics under real traffic traces.

Appendix: quick routing decision cheat-sheet (one-liners)
- Need compute isolation and predictable SLO -> Dedicated.
- Need compliance and egress control -> Private.
- Need fastest time-to-market and lower cost -> Hosted.

Notes to reviewers:
- This doc intentionally mixes concrete operational checks with heuristics. Please add missing edge cases (e.g., GPU SKU heterogeneity, model-compatibility constraints across quantized variants) and link any relevant RFCs or Confluence pages.

   - For Hosted/Dedicated, central control plane with regional zones is default.

Checklist: configuration knobs to choose per deployment mode
- Tenant identity surface: API key only vs. API key + tenant-id headers. Default: all modes use tenant-id header.
- Routing affinity: region affinity vs. stateless routing. Default: Hosted -> region-aware but stateless; Dedicated -> affinity to capacity pool; Private -> customer-defined affinity.
- Egress footprint: shared NAT vs. per-tenant egress IPs. Default: Hosted -> shared; Dedicated/Private -> per-tenant or per-commit egress.
- KV/prefix caching: global shared cache vs. tenant-scoped cache. Default: isolate tokenized caches per-tenant for privacy, share read-only model artifacts.
- Fallback policy: model-variant fallback (cheaper/quantized) vs. capacity-tier fallback. Default: Hosted -> automatic fallback; Dedicated -> controlled fallback (owner approval); Private -> disabled unless explicitly configured.

Isolation boundary patterns (concrete examples)
- Logical-only isolation (Hosted, default):
  - Single serving runtime clusters per region.
  - Namespaces and RBAC isolate metadata, but compute and kernels are shared.
  - Advantages: cost efficiency, fast routing, easier autoscaling.
  - Risks: noisy neighbor; require strong telemetry + dynamic throttles.

- Tenant pool isolation (Dedicated):
  - Dedicated GPU pools pinned to customers or groups.
  - Control plane routes by pool-id, runtime enforces hard tenant fence (cgroups, container-level).
  - Advantages: predictable throughput, easier per-tenant SLA.
  - Risks: underutilization unless burst/overflow patterns allowed.

- Full private isolation (Private/VPC or on-prem):
  - Customer controls compute/networking. Control plane either federated or local agent manages runtime.
  - Service-level routing occurs inside customer's network; Redwood control plane issues manifests.
  - Advantages: compliance, full egress control.
  - Risks: operational overhead, slower feature rollout.

Control plane responsibilities by mode
- Hosted control plane (centralized):
  - Global model catalog, billing, usage analytics, routing policies, and rollout control.
  - Maintains regional proxies for latency-sensitive routing decisions.
  - Responsible for automatic fallback rules and cross-region reroute.

- Dedicated control plane (hybrid):
  - Central control plane owns policy + telemetry; local control-plane agents own capacity placement and low-level routing within dedicated pools.
  - Policy enforcement: central policy evaluates tenant eligibility for pool; local agent enforces affinity and local failover.

- Private control plane (federated/local):
  - Minimal central visibility (billing/health ping optional).
  - Local control plane must support model pinning, security policy enforcement, and optional sync of audit logs to Redwood.

Routing policies & examples (pseudo rules)
- Policy: region-affinity-with-fallback
  - rule: prefer region == tenant.preferred_region; if capacity < 80% then route to nearest-region with compatible model; if latency projection > SLO then fallback to compressed-variant.
- Policy: strict-isolation
  - rule: only route to pools tagged tenant_id == X and do not use shared caches; reject if no available capacity.
- Policy: cost-saver
  - rule: route to quantized variant during off-peak hours unless quality degradation alert triggers.

Multi-region failover matrix (short)
- Hosted: cross-region routing enabled by default; failover latency includes control-plane decision + regional proxy hop. SLA: best-effort regional failover within 30s for degraded regions.
- Dedicated: failover within same commit/tenant pool family; if failover to different pool required, apply canary validation for 5–10 minutes.
- Private: failover decisions are customer-configured; Redwood recommends a primary/secondary region manifest with health probes.

Telemetry and tagging requirements (must-have for safe shared-hosted)
- Token-level cost attribution tags (model, quantization, route)
- Per-request tenancy tag (tenant_id, pool_id, region)
- Noise detection: continuous scoring of per-tenant token variance and latency spikes to trigger throttles or suggested pool move

Operational runbook (onboard-to-live in 6 steps)
1) Customer intake questionnaire (chooses isolation level, region, egress, compliance needs).
2) Default routing profile generation (we assign region affinity + fallback).
3) Capacity plan and cost estimation (if Dedicated, reserve N GPUs or autoscale schedule).
4) Local/networking config (for Private: VPC peering, NAT egress mapping, control-plane agent install).
5) Smoke tests: end-to-end latency, region-failover drill, egress verification, and model-variant degradation check.
6) Production cutover with canary: start at 5% traffic for 1 hour, validate telemetry, accelerate to 100% if green.

Incident playbook highlights (routing-related incidents)
- Symptom: wide latency spike in a region
  - Immediately: instantiate temporary cross-region routing override to nearest healthy region, toggle autoscaling up for affected pools.
  - If owned pool: move affected tenants to secondary dedicated pool; notify CS and tenant.
- Symptom: noisy tenant consuming disproportionate tokens
  - Immediately: apply dynamic throttling, reduce max concurrency, open support ticket with tenant.
- Symptom: egress IP requirement violated
  - Immediately: rollback to previous routing manifest, adjust NAT mapping; for Private, coordinate with customer networking.

Trade-offs and recommended defaults (summary)
- Default for SMB/no-compliance: Hosted with region affinity, automatic fallback, shared egress, and per-tenant logical isolation.
- Default for high-throughput/SLA customers: Dedicated with tenant pool isolation, per-tenant egress, and controlled fallback policies.
- Default for regulated/data-residency: Private with federated control plane and customer-managed routing.

Open questions to resolve (for engineering roadmap)
- What is the exact API surface for tenant-level local routing hints? (low-latency header vs control-plane manifest?)
- How to represent multi-tenant cache sharing in telemetry without leaking PII?
- Run cost simulation tool: need a calculator to show Dedicated idle vs burst economics under real traffic traces.

Appendix: quick routing decision cheat-sheet (one-liners)
- Need compute isolation and predictable SLO -> Dedicated.
- Need compliance and egress control -> Private.
- Need fastest time-to-market and lower cost -> Hosted.

Notes to reviewers:
- This doc intentionally mixes concrete operational checks with heuristics. Please add missing edge cases (e.g., GPU SKU heterogeneity, model-compatibility constraints across quantized variants) and link any relevant RFCs or Confluence pages.

source.title: Straggler mitigation experiments — replica hedging + jittered prefetch (scratch)
source.content:
Goal:
- Quick, informal log of experiments aimed at reducing p99 tail caused by a small fraction of long-running model requests (stragglers). Focused on two lightweight mitigations: replica hedging (dispatch a second replica after delay) and jittered prefetch (start nvtx-prefetch for KV cache with randomized offset).

Background/context:
- Running 8x A100 pool, steady-state QPS ~120 for the test shard. Baseline request profile: 90% short (<=128 tokens), 9% medium (129-512), 1% long (>1024). Long requests cause KV cache churn / kernel stalls and inflate p99 by ~3x.

Experimental design (quick):
- Workload: replay production arrival traces sampled for 60m window (diurnal mid-peak), preserving interarrival variability.
- Baseline config (A): single-replica per request, no prefetch.
- Hedging config (B): issue hedged request to second replica if primary not started producing tokens after T_hedge = 60ms. Cancel loser when winner returns first token.
- Prefetch config (C): jittered prefetch of KV/cache: when request accepted, start async prefetch with randomized delay uniform(0, 10ms) to avoid synchronized cache thrash across workers.
- Combined (D): B + C.
- Each run: 30m warmup + 30m measured. Repeat x3 for variance check.

Metrics captured: p50/p95/p99 (latency to first token and end-to-end 95th/99th), tail-penalty (p99/p50), observed QPS, additional GPU token compute (as proxy for cost), request cancellation rate, duplicate-run fraction (hedging overhead).

Raw-ish aggregated results (median of 3 runs):
- A (baseline): p50_first=18ms, p95_first=78ms, p99_first=312ms; p50_e2e=210ms, p95_e2e=430ms, p99_e2e=1280ms; QPS=119; duplicate_frac=0; cost_delta=0%
- B (hedging 60ms): p50_first=19ms, p95_first=62ms, p99_first=115ms; p50_e2e=215ms, p95_e2e=380ms, p99_e2e=520ms; QPS=117; duplicate_frac=3.8%; cost_delta=+4.5%
- C (jittered prefetch): p50_first=17ms, p95_first=70ms, p99_first=210ms; p50_e2e=205ms, p95_e2e=400ms, p99_e2e=920ms; QPS=118; duplicate_frac=0; cost_delta=+0.8% (slightly higher kernel utilization)
- D (B+C): p50_first=18ms, p95_first=58ms, p99_first=98ms; p50_e2e=212ms, p95_e2e=360ms, p99_e2e=440ms; QPS=116; duplicate_frac=4.1%; cost_delta=+5.2%

Observations / quick interpretation:
- Hedging gives the largest p99 improvement for first-token latency (baseline 312ms -> 115ms); reasonable overhead (~4% duplicate). Useful when p99-first-token critical for tail-sensitive services (chat UX).
- Jittered prefetch reduces some tail for end-to-end p99 by smoothing cache pressure. Not as effective as hedging alone, but low overhead. It decreases some of the super-long tails likely caused by synchronized cache misses.
- Combined strategy shows additive benefits: hedging mitigates pathological long-running single-replica stalls; prefetch reduces the incidence of mid-tail slowdowns (hence lower p95).
- QPS drop is small (~2-3 QPS) likely due to cancellation bookkeeping and small increase in GPU kernel churn.

Failure modes / things to watch:
- Hedging can exacerbate bursty GPU load in overload conditions; need global admission control or hedging-limited tokens per-node to avoid cascading.
- Duplicate_frac spikes when primary replicas are placed on same physical host; ensure hedged replica selection prefers different host/queue.
- Jitter range tuning matters: too large -> loses cache priming benefits; too small -> synchrony remains.

Next actionable items (short list):
1) Run hedging placement test: ensure hedges go to different NUMA/socket/host more often. Quick Sim: route hedges to host with different active-request-count and compare duplicate_frac. (Owner: Maya)
2) Explore adaptive hedging threshold: determine T_hedge per tenant based on historical first-token CDF. Could save duplicates for low-tail tenants. (Owner: Ravi)
3) Add a lightweight per-replica hedging budget token and integrate with autoscaler to avoid burst amplification. (Owner: Ethan)
4) Stress test under overloaded conditions (QPS pushes to 130-160) to see cascade behavior. (Owner: Ethan/Ravi)
5) Capture NVTX traces for 10 long requests to figure out whether stalls are kernel-blocked or waiting on disk/network.

Notes to self / hypotheses to validate later:
- Hypothesis: many p99 events originate from a small set of models/sequence-length combos that trigger a slow kernel path. Hedging works because the cold kernel invocation on another card may hit a different kernel path or warm KV cache.
- Hypothesis: jittered prefetch reduces tail by desynchronizing aggressive kernel-prep across co-located workers.

Quick reproducibility commands (notes):
- Trace generation: ./tools/replay_trace.py --input traces/mid-peak.json --scale 1.0 --seed 42 --duration 3600 --output /tmp/replay.sock
- Run harness: ./experiments/run_experiment.sh --config configs/hedge-jitter.yaml --replay /tmp/replay.sock --out /tmp/results/hedge-jitter

Anything missing: add memory-pressure metrics per-GPU and host-level rtt histograms next run. Also compare hedging at different thresholds (30/60/120ms) and measure duplicate cost curve.

End of scratchpad (keep this as a living note).

source.title: GPU SKU allocation scenario planner
source.content:
Purpose:
This working doc defines a scenario-driven SKU allocation and reorder-policy model for Redwood's GPU fleet across regions. It is intended to complement the existing inventory and procurement trackers by: 1) making trade-offs explicit when lead-times spike, 2) allocating constrained SKUs to the highest-value workloads, and 3) producing clear reorder triggers and cadence for procurement ops.

Context / why this is new: current trackers are delivery-focused (orders, commits) but lack a reusable scenario layer to tell finance, SRE, and product how we should shift SKU mix under supply stress. This is the team-level operational playbook for Q2–Q4 2024.

Key assumptions (summary):
- Demand baseline: 20k GPU-hours/day (global) as of Jan 2024, projected growth 18% q/q under baseline product growth signals.
- SKU classes considered: A100-80GB (training heavy), A100-40GB (mixed), H100-80GB (preferred for LLM inference), L40s (inference cost-efficient), RTX-class (dev/test).
- Procurement lead-times (current median): H100: 16 weeks, A100-80GB: 12 weeks, L40: 10 weeks, RTX: 4 weeks. Variability +/- 25% under supply stress.
- Cross-region transfer: feasible but costly; plan for 3–5% fleet transfer per month in emergencies.

Inputs / data sources:
- Fleet utilization dashboard (internal): avg daily GPU-hours by SKU and region
- Procurement ledger: active orders and expected ship weeks
- Vendor SLA / historical lead-time log (attachments)
- Product roadmap signals (major model launches -> expected token throughput increase)

Scenario definitions (high level):
- Conservative (stress): demand +35%, lead-times +40%, budget constrained -> prioritize inference (H100/L40) capacity and hold new training orders
- Baseline (expected): demand +18% q/q, lead-times stable +/-10%
- Aggressive (growth): demand +55%, budget flexible, acceptable to take more RTX/dev capacity to support faster iteration

Allocation logic (how we decide SKU distribution):
1) Define workload priority buckets:
   - P0: production inference (LLM endpoints, latency-sensitive)
   - P1: nearline/async inference and critical retraining (nightly retrains)
   - P2: experimental training and dev (non-critical)
2) Map SKUs to buckets by cost/latency tradeoff: H100 -> P0/P1, A100-80GB -> P1/P2, L40 -> P0 (cost-optimized inference), RTX -> P2
3) When supply-constrained, satisfy fleet targets in order: P0 -> P1 -> P2.

Sample allocation table (illustrative numbers for planning):
- Global target fleet (baseline end-Q2): 2,200 x H100, 900 x A100-80GB, 1,800 x L40, 600 x RTX
- Conservative reallocation (if lead-time shock +40% and demand +35%): reduce fresh A100-80GB purchases by 60%, re-route 30% of L40 order volume to H100 where available, increase preemptive cross-region buffer to 10% of H100 fleet.

Safety stock and reorder point methodology (plaintext formulas):
- avg_daily_consumption = 20,000 gpu-hours/day / effective_gpu_hours_per_unit (depends on SKU). Use SKU-specific consumption tables (see linked artifacts).
- safety_stock_hours = z * stddev_daily_hours * lead_time_days (z = 1.65 for 95% service level recommended for P0)
- reorder_point_hours = avg_daily_consumption * lead_time_days + safety_stock_hours
- convert hours to units: reorder_point_units = ceil(reorder_point_hours / effective_gpu_hours_per_unit)

Example calculation (H100, P0):
- effective_gpu_hours_per_unit = 24 * utilization_target (0.85) * performance_factor -> use 20 usable hours/day for planning
- avg_daily_consumption_H100 = 8,000 hours/day (P0 portion)
- lead_time_days = 16 weeks * 7 = 112 days
- stddev_daily_hours (last 90d) = 1,200 hours
- safety_stock_hours = 1.65 * 1,200 * 112 = ~221,760 hours
- reorder_point_hours = 8,000 * 112 + 221,760 = 1,137,760 hours -> reorder_point_units ≈ 56 H100 units (rounded)
Notes: this is illustrative; ops should run the live spreadsheet to get real numbers per SKU/region.

Procurement cadence & playbook recommendations:
- Maintain rolling 6-month procurement pipeline with weekly commits reviewed by procurement ops.
- For H100 and critical inference SKUs: stagger orders monthly with overlapping ship windows to avoid full exposure to single lead-time event.
- For training-heavy SKUs (A100-80GB): favor quarterly bulk orders when spot price advantageous; include clauses for partial cancellations or substitutions.
- Small-dev SKUs (RTX): opportunistic ordering on 4-week cadence.

Vendor & lead-time risk mitigations:
- Multi-vendor strategy: aim for at least two approved vendors per SKU class; maintain a standing PO line for last-mile substitutions.
- Contractual SLA: add penalty & expedite clauses for H100 shipments in enterprise agreements (work with legal).
- Secondary market playbook: pre-approved brokers + approved price thresholds for urgent buys; cap usage to 12% of fleet to avoid reliability unknowns.

Region-specific notes (NA / EU / APAC):
- NA: primary reserve for H100 capacity; historically lowest lead-time variance. Keep 40% of H100 global stock here.
- EU: prioritize A100 and L40 for local latency-sensitive inference to avoid cross-region egress. Maintain 30% of L40 stock in EU.
- APAC: higher procurement lead-times historically; keep a higher safety buffer (15%) and leverage dedicated private-deploy partners for urgent inflight capacity.

Operational runbook items (next steps / owners):
- Marco: assemble live SKU consumption sheet and validate effective_gpu_hours_per_unit by 2024-03-14 (JIRA-INFRA-3189).
- Lina: negotiate H100 expedite clause template with legal by 2024-03-21.
- Priya: validate vendor lead-time variance distribution and recommend two secondary vendors per SKU by 2024-03-28.
- Ops: run scenario model weekly and publish a one-slide summary to procurement sync every Friday.

Decision triggers (examples):
- If lead-time for H100 > 21 weeks OR order fill rate drops below 65% for two consecutive weeks -> shift budget: pause A100 bulk purchase and reallocate 70% of capital to H100 emergency buys.
- If global utilization > 92% for P0 for 14 days -> expedite next H100 tranche and increase safety_stock z-factor to 2.0.

Open questions / outstanding data gaps:
- Need SKU-specific effective_gpu_hours_per_unit validated across model families (LLM vs vision).
- Historical vendor cancellation rates and partial-ship frequencies (ops to extract from procurement ledger).
- Precise cost-per-GPU-hour modeling to tie SKU allocation to unit economics (finance input required).

Appendix: quick-links & references (work-in-progress)
- Procurement ledger (internal sheet): CONFLUENCE-GB/gpu-proc-ledger
- Fleet utilization dashboard: internal-dashboard/fleet-utilization
- Related docs: gpu-inventory-commit-schedule-by-region, gpu-procurement-pipeline-tranches

This remains a working doc — update when new vendor lead-time samples arrive or the product roadmap changes. Please add comments inline and flag any numbers you cannot reproduce from the ledger.

Purpose:
This working doc defines a scenario-driven SKU allocation and reorder-policy model for Redwood's GPU fleet across regions. It is intended to complement the existing inventory and procurement trackers by: 1) making trade-offs explicit when lead-times spike, 2) allocating constrained SKUs to the highest-value workloads, and 3) producing clear reorder triggers and cadence for procurement ops.

Context / why this is new: current trackers are delivery-focused (orders, commits) but lack a reusable scenario layer to tell finance, SRE, and product how we should shift SKU mix under supply stress. This is the team-level operational playbook for Q2–Q4 2024.

Key assumptions (summary):
- Demand baseline: 20k GPU-hours/day (global) as of Jan 2024, projected growth 18% q/q under baseline product growth signals.
- SKU classes considered: A100-80GB (training heavy), A100-40GB (mixed), H100-80GB (preferred for LLM inference), L40s (inference cost-efficient), RTX-class (dev/test).
- Procurement lead-times (current median): H100: 16 weeks, A100-80GB: 12 weeks, L40: 10 weeks, RTX: 4 weeks. Variability +/- 25% under supply stress.
- Cross-region transfer: feasible but costly; plan for 3–5% fleet transfer per month in emergencies.

Inputs / data sources:
- Fleet utilization dashboard (internal): avg daily GPU-hours by SKU and region
- Procurement ledger: active orders and expected ship weeks
- Vendor SLA / historical lead-time log (attachments)
- Product roadmap signals (major model launches -> expected token throughput increase)

Scenario definitions (high level):
- Conservative (stress): demand +35%, lead-times +40%, budget constrained -> prioritize inference (H100/L40) capacity and hold new training orders
- Baseline (expected): demand +18% q/q, lead-times stable +/-10%
- Aggressive (growth): demand +55%, budget flexible, acceptable to take more RTX/dev capacity to support faster iteration

Allocation logic (how we decide SKU distribution):
1) Define workload priority buckets:
   - P0: production inference (LLM endpoints, latency-sensitive)
   - P1: nearline/async inference and critical retraining (nightly retrains)
   - P2: experimental training and dev (non-critical)
2) Map SKUs to buckets by cost/latency tradeoff: H100 -> P0/P1, A100-80GB -> P1/P2, L40 -> P0 (cost-optimized inference), RTX -> P2
3) When supply-constrained, satisfy fleet targets in order: P0 -> P1 -> P2.

Sample allocation table (illustrative numbers for planning):
- Global target fleet (baseline end-Q2): 2,200 x H100, 900 x A100-80GB, 1,800 x L40, 600 x RTX
- Conservative reallocation (if lead-time shock +40% and demand +35%): reduce fresh A100-80GB purchases by 60%, re-route 30% of L40 order volume to H100 where available, increase preemptive cross-region buffer to 10% of H100 fleet.

Safety stock and reorder point methodology (plaintext formulas):
- avg_daily_consumption = 20,000 gpu-hours/day / effective_gpu_hours_per_unit (depends on SKU). Use SKU-specific consumption tables (see linked artifacts).
- safety_stock_hours = z * stddev_daily_hours * lead_time_days (z = 1.65 for 95% service level recommended for P0)
- reorder_point_hours = avg_daily_consumption * lead_time_days + safety_stock_hours
- convert hours to units: reorder_point_units = ceil(reorder_point_hours / effective_gpu_hours_per_unit)

Example calculation (H100, P0):
- effective_gpu_hours_per_unit = 24 * utilization_target (0.85) * performance_factor -> use 20 usable hours/day for planning
- avg_daily_consumption_H100 = 8,000 hours/day (P0 portion)
- lead_time_days = 16 weeks * 7 = 112 days
- stddev_daily_hours (last 90d) = 1,200 hours
- safety_stock_hours = 1.65 * 1,200 * 112 = ~221,760 hours
- reorder_point_hours = 8,000 * 112 + 221,760 = 1,137,760 hours -> reorder_point_units ≈ 56 H100 units (rounded)
Notes: this is illustrative; ops should run the live spreadsheet to get real numbers per SKU/region.

Procurement cadence & playbook recommendations:
- Maintain rolling 6-month procurement pipeline with weekly commits reviewed by procurement ops.
- For H100 and critical inference SKUs: stagger orders monthly with overlapping ship windows to avoid full exposure to single lead-time event.
- For training-heavy SKUs (A100-80GB): favor quarterly bulk orders when spot price advantageous; include clauses for partial cancellations or substitutions.
- Small-dev SKUs (RTX): opportunistic ordering on 4-week cadence.

Vendor & lead-time risk mitigations:
- Multi-vendor strategy: aim for at least two approved vendors per SKU class; maintain a standing PO line for last-mile substitutions.
- Contractual SLA: add penalty & expedite clauses for H100 shipments in enterprise agreements (work with legal).
- Secondary market playbook: pre-approved brokers + approved price thresholds for urgent buys; cap usage to 12% of fleet to avoid reliability unknowns.

Region-specific notes (NA / EU / APAC):
- NA: primary reserve for H100 capacity; historically lowest lead-time variance. Keep 40% of H100 global stock here.
- EU: prioritize A100 and L40 for local latency-sensitive inference to avoid cross-region egress. Maintain 30% of L40 stock in EU.
- APAC: higher procurement lead-times historically; keep a higher safety buffer (15%) and leverage dedicated private-deploy partners for urgent inflight capacity.

Operational runbook items (next steps / owners):
- Marco: assemble live SKU consumption sheet and validate effective_gpu_hours_per_unit by 2024-03-14 (JIRA-INFRA-3189).
- Lina: negotiate H100 expedite clause template with legal by 2024-03-21.
- Priya: validate vendor lead-time variance distribution and recommend two secondary vendors per SKU by 2024-03-28.
- Ops: run scenario model weekly and publish a one-slide summary to procurement sync every Friday.

Decision triggers (examples):
- If lead-time for H100 > 21 weeks OR order fill rate drops below 65% for two consecutive weeks -> shift budget: pause A100 bulk purchase and reallocate 70% of capital to H100 emergency buys.
- If global utilization > 92% for P0 for 14 days -> expedite next H100 tranche and increase safety_stock z-factor to 2.0.

Open questions / outstanding data gaps:
- Need SKU-specific effective_gpu_hours_per_unit validated across model families (LLM vs vision).
- Historical vendor cancellation rates and partial-ship frequencies (ops to extract from procurement ledger).
- Precise cost-per-GPU-hour modeling to tie SKU allocation to unit economics (finance input required).

Appendix: quick-links & references (work-in-progress)
- Procurement ledger (internal sheet): CONFLUENCE-GB/gpu-proc-ledger
- Fleet utilization dashboard: internal-dashboard/fleet-utilization
- Related docs: gpu-inventory-commit-schedule-by-region, gpu-procurement-pipeline-tranches

This remains a working doc — update when new vendor lead-time samples arrive or the product roadmap changes. Please add comments inline and flag any numbers you cannot reproduce from the ledger.
